title: RecordCompletion computes content hash ignoring existing authoring outputs
description:
In execution.go:115, RecordCompletion calls contenthash.Spec(intent, newStage, priority, complexity, nil) with nil for the authoring outputs map. This drops any spark/shape/specify/decompose outputs from the hash computation, producing an incorrect content_hash. The recomputeContentHash helper at lifecycle.go:419 shows the correct approach: read all four output columns and pass them as a map. Every spec that goes through RecordCompletion will have its content_hash set to a value that disagrees with what GetSpec and all subsequent hash checks would compute, silently breaking drift detection for all downstream DEPENDS_ON edges and any content-hash-based idempotency checks.

File: internal/storage/postgres/execution.go, line 115.

Fix: replace the manual contenthash.Spec call with s.recomputeContentHash(txCtx, slug) (as done in TransitionStage, AmendSpec, LifecycleAmendSpec, etc.) and remove the inline UPDATE of content_hash from the spec UPDATE statement. Alternatively, fetch the authoring output columns in the same SELECT and pass them properly to contenthash.Spec.

title: resolveNodeRef silently swallows DB errors, returns minimal stub NodeRef
description: graph.go:533-556 — resolveNodeRef queries specs/decisions/slices and on ANY DB error returns storage.NodeRef{Slug: slug} (an empty stub with no label or stage). The stub is then indistinguishable from a node that genuinely exists but has no label. This stub propagates into GetDependenciesWithEdgeData results and ultimately into bundle dependencies, meaning callers see a malformed NodeRef instead of an error. Callers that check Label or Stage will silently see blank values. Fix: return (NodeRef, error) and propagate DB errors; only swallow pgx.ErrNoRows as a legitimate 'not found'.

title: lookupContentHash silently swallows DB errors, returns empty string
description: graph.go:515-528 — lookupContentHash queries the DB and on ANY error (network partition, connection drop, syntax error) silently discards it and returns empty string. The comment says 'missing hash is not an error', but this conflates the 'node has no hash yet' case with 'DB query failed'. An empty string returned under error conditions then propagates as a silent zero-value for content_hash_at_link on DEPENDS_ON edges, causing AddEdge to record a blank baseline and making every subsequent drift check report false drift. Fix: distinguish pgx.ErrNoRows (legitimately no hash) from other errors. On non-NoRows errors, propagate the error up to AddEdge.

title: concepts/drift.md worked example uses nonexistent 'specgraph depend' command and wrong --note flag
description:
site/docs/concepts/drift.md Worked Example section contains two errors:

1. Line ~2519: 'specgraph depend payment-service --on auth-tokens'
   - This command does not exist. The correct command is:
     specgraph edge add payment-service auth-tokens --type depends_on
   - There is no 'depend' subcommand in cmd/specgraph/

2. Line ~2526: 'specgraph update auth-tokens --note "Add refresh_token field..."'
   - The flag is --notes (plural), not --note
   - Confirmed in cmd/specgraph/spec.go: 'updateCmd.Flags().StringVar(&updateNotes, "notes", ...)'

Suggested fix:
  # Create the dependency
  specgraph edge add payment-service auth-tokens --type depends_on

  # Update the upstream spec
  specgraph update auth-tokens --notes "Add refresh_token field to contract"

title: cli-cookbook.md recipe 4 uses nonexistent commands: specgraph add-edge, remove-edge, depend, and wrong edge type format
description:
site/docs/guides/cli-cookbook.md Recipe 4 (Detect and resolve drift) uses three nonexistent commands:

1. Line ~2967: 'specgraph add-edge auth-service --to token-storage --type depends-on'
   - The command is 'specgraph edge add <from> <to> [--type depends_on]'
   - Flag format is depends_on (underscore), not depends-on (hyphen)
   - Confirmed in cmd/specgraph/edge.go: edgeAddCmd uses 'edge add <from-slug> <to-slug>'

2. Line ~3035: 'specgraph remove-edge auth-service --to api-gateway --type depends-on'
   - The command is 'specgraph edge remove <from> <to>'
   - Same edge type format issue

These are documented in cli-reference.md correctly ('edge add', 'edge remove') but the cookbook examples are inconsistent.

Suggested fix:
  specgraph edge add auth-service token-storage --type depends_on
  specgraph edge remove auth-service api-gateway --type depends_on

title: cli-cookbook.md recipe 6 uses wrong flags: --assignee/--ttl/--message/--description do not exist on claim/report commands
description:
site/docs/guides/cli-cookbook.md Recipe 6 (Manage execution lifecycle) contains multiple wrong flag names that would immediately fail at runtime:

1. Line ~3053: 'specgraph claim auth-service --assignee alice --ttl 30m'
   - Actual flags: --agent alice --duration 30m (not --assignee / --ttl)
   - Confirmed in cmd/specgraph/claim.go: claimCmd uses --agent and --duration

2. Line ~3057: 'specgraph report-progress auth-service --message "..."'
   - Missing required --agent flag; command requires both --agent and --message

3. Line ~3060: 'specgraph report-blocker auth-service --message "Waiting on..."'
   - Actual flag is --description, not --message (reportBlockerCmd.description)
   - Confirmed in cmd/specgraph/report.go

4. Line ~3065: 'specgraph report-completion auth-service'
   - Missing required --agent flag

Suggested fix: Update all four commands in Recipe 6 to use --agent instead of --assignee, --duration instead of --ttl, and --description instead of --message for report-blocker. Add --agent to report-progress and report-completion calls.

title: Missing HTTP request body size limits and server timeouts
description: http.Server has no ReadTimeout, WriteTimeout, IdleTimeout, or MaxBytesReader. Protobuf deserialization happens before handler validation, so multi-MB payloads are fully buffered. Fix: add timeouts to http.Server, use connect.WithReadMaxBytes(4<<20) handler option. Files: cmd/specgraph/serve.go

title: Unauthenticated /api/projects endpoint bypasses auth interceptor
description: /api/projects is a plain http.HandleFunc registered directly on the mux, bypassing the ConnectRPC auth interceptor. Any unauthenticated caller can enumerate all project slugs even when API keys are configured. Fix: wrap in auth middleware or proxy through a ConnectRPC service. Files: internal/server/api_handler.go, cmd/specgraph/serve.go
close_reason: Already fixed in PR #694 (wave 1). RegisterAPIHandlers requires authMW param, wraps handler, panics if nil. Tests cover 401/200/passthrough.

title: Raw storage errors leaked to API clients in ~30 handler call sites
description: Many handlers pass raw Memgraph driver errors to connect.NewError(CodeInternal, err), leaking Cypher query fragments and DB internals. The stageError()/lifecycleError() pattern exists but isn't applied uniformly. Fix: apply lifecycleError() pattern to all handlers — log full error server-side, return generic 'internal error' to client. Files: internal/server/spec_handler.go, constitution_handler.go, graph_handler.go, claim_handler.go, execution_handler.go, decision_handler.go, analytical_pass_handler.go

title: UpdateSpec allows unrestricted stage mutation, bypassing state machine
description: UpdateSpec RPC accepts optional stage string and writes it directly to the graph without transition validation. Any caller can set arbitrary stage values, bypassing the authoring funnel and lifecycle RPCs. Fix: remove stage from UpdateSpec's mutable fields — stage transitions belong exclusively to Authoring and Lifecycle RPCs. Files: internal/server/spec_handler.go, internal/storage/memgraph/memgraph.go

title: SpecHandler.CreateSpec maps ErrSpecAlreadyExists to CodeInternal instead of CodeAlreadyExists
description:
The storage layer now correctly returns ErrSpecAlreadyExists on duplicate slug, and the AuthoringHandler (Spark) maps it to connect.CodeAlreadyExists. However, SpecHandler.CreateSpec at internal/server/spec_handler.go:56-57 does not check for ErrSpecAlreadyExists — it falls through to a generic connect.CodeInternal response. Clients calling CreateSpec directly (not via Spark) will receive a 500-equivalent instead of a 409-equivalent, making the error non-retryable and opaque.

No test in spec_handler_test.go covers this case. A test like TestSpecHandler_CreateSpec_DuplicateSlug should verify that calling CreateSpec twice with the same slug returns connect.CodeAlreadyExists.

Fix: add errors.Is(err, storage.ErrSpecAlreadyExists) guard in SpecHandler.CreateSpec (same pattern as AuthoringHandler.Spark line 85-87).
close_reason: Fixed: slug_key constraint + error sanitization + existsParams reuse

title: StoreFindings: finding.PassType zero value causes wrong pass_type stored in graph
description:
In internal/server/analytical_pass_handler.go (lines 821-827), the handler constructs domain findings without setting PassType:

    domain[i] = storage.AnalyticalFinding{
        Severity:   sev,
        Summary:    f.Summary,
        ...
        // PassType is NOT set — zero value ""
    }

These are then passed to store.StoreFindings(ctx, msg.Slug, pt, domain).

In internal/storage/memgraph/findings.go (line ~2292), the CREATE query uses f.PassType from the individual element:
    "pass_type": string(f.PassType),   // always "" because handler never set it

The correct pass type (the validated pt variable) is only used for the delete step but NOT for node creation. Every Finding node will be persisted with pass_type="" in the graph, making ListFindings by pass type return nothing.

Fix: either set PassType on each domain element in the handler before calling StoreFindings, OR have the memgraph implementation always stamp the method-level passType parameter on each node (ignoring the element field). The interface contract should be clarified: does the passType param win, or the element field? Given passType is validated at the method boundary, the method param should be authoritative. The simplest fix is in the handler:

    domain[i] = storage.AnalyticalFinding{
        PassType:   pt,   // ADD THIS
        Severity:   sev,
        ...
    }

title: AnalyticalPassService RPCs missing from auth permissions registry
description:
The three new RPCs (RunAnalyticalPass, StoreFindings, ListFindings) are registered on the mux in serve.go but have no entries in internal/auth/permissions.go rpcPermissions map, and are absent from the allProcedures list in internal/auth/permissions_test.go.

The project uses a permissions table to gate every RPC behind a required auth scope. The test in permissions_test.go asserts that every registered procedure has a permissions entry. Because the new AnalyticalPassService procedures are neither in rpcPermissions nor in allProcedures, the permissions test will currently pass (they are simply absent from the expected list), but the actual runtime effect is that these endpoints have no authorization requirement enforced by the auth middleware.

Affected file: internal/auth/permissions.go (missing additions) and internal/auth/permissions_test.go (missing additions).

Fix: Add entries to rpcPermissions, for example:
  specgraphv1connect.AnalyticalPassServiceRunAnalyticalPassProcedure: "analytical_pass:read",
  specgraphv1connect.AnalyticalPassServiceStoreFindingsProcedure:     "analytical_pass:write",
  specgraphv1connect.AnalyticalPassServiceListFindingsProcedure:      "analytical_pass:read",

And add all three to allProcedures in permissions_test.go.
close_reason: Duplicate of spgr-bp7.1 (same auth permissions finding)

title: New AnalyticalPassService RPCs missing from auth permissions map
description: internal/auth/permissions.go has a rpcPermissions map that gates every RPC. The diff shows CheckViolationProcedure was removed from this map, but the three new AnalyticalPassService procedures (RunAnalyticalPassProcedure, StoreFindingsProcedure, ListFindingsProcedure) were never added. The corresponding permissions_test.go allProcedures list also has no entries for the new service. Without entries in rpcPermissions, callers either get blocked by the catch-all or bypass auth entirely, depending on the middleware's default — either way it is a security/correctness gap. A test asserting that every registered procedure has a permissions entry would have caught this on first compile.
close_reason: Already fixed — permissions map (permissions.go:66-69) and test coverage (permissions_test.go:69-72) both include all three AnalyticalPassService RPCs.

title: Three new AnalyticalPassService RPCs have no entries in rpcPermissions, bypassing auth entirely
description: The three new RPCs (RunAnalyticalPass, StoreFindings, ListFindings) registered in serve.go are not present in the rpcPermissions map in internal/auth/permissions.go. The PR diff shows permissions.go was modified (to remove the old CheckViolation entry) but no entries were added for the new service. Depending on how the auth middleware handles an unknown procedure, the RPCs may be accessible without any authentication or authorization check. If the middleware denies unknowns, this would cause all three RPCs to return 403 for legitimate callers; if it allows them, all three are unauthenticated. Either outcome is a security defect. Fix: add appropriate permission entries, e.g. AnalyticalPassServiceRunAnalyticalPassProcedure: "findings:read", AnalyticalPassServiceStoreFindingsProcedure: "findings:write", AnalyticalPassServiceListFindingsProcedure: "findings:read". Also add these to the allProcedures list in permissions_test.go.

title: quickstart.md: CLI equivalent blocks show non-existent flags for all authoring commands
description:
The 'CLI equivalent' collapsible blocks in site/docs/quickstart.md (diff lines 1900-1974) show flags that do not exist in the actual CLI:

spark block (line 1900-1907): shows --signal, --scope-sniff, --kill-test. Actual cmd/specgraph/spark.go only accepts --seed.

shape block (line 1923-1930): shows --scope-in, --scope-out, --approaches. Actual cmd/specgraph/shape.go only accepts --json-file.

specify block (line 1948-1953): shows --interface, --criteria, --invariant. Actual cmd/specgraph/specify.go only accepts --json-file.

decompose block (line 1969-1974): shows --strategy, --slice. Actual cmd/specgraph/decompose.go only accepts --json-file.

A developer copying any of these commands will get 'unknown flag' errors. This is the most harmful accuracy issue in the entire PR since the quickstart is the primary onboarding document.

Fix: Replace the fabricated flag syntax with the actual interface. For spark: only --seed is accepted. For shape/specify/decompose: the CLI accepts --json-file pointing to a JSON file containing the serialized output struct; show an example of the JSON file approach, or note that these stages are best done via Claude Code skills.
close_reason: Fixed CLI flags to use actual --seed and --json-file

title: quickstart.md: server port is wrong — localhost:7600 should be 127.0.0.1:7890
description:
site/docs/quickstart.md (diff line 1839) states the server listens at `http://localhost:7600` by default. The actual default from internal/config/global.go (globalDefaults) is `http://127.0.0.1:7890`. This will cause a new user to point their client at the wrong address and get connection failures with no obvious explanation.

Fix: Replace `http://localhost:7600` with `http://127.0.0.1:7890` (or just state 'port 7890').

title: .beads/.beads-credential-key commits a binary credential to the repository
description:
File: .beads/.beads-credential-key (new file, line 1-8 of diff)

The PR adds .beads/.beads-credential-key which contains binary credential data (visible in diff as garbage bytes). This is a machine-local secret used by the bd CLI. It must not be committed to the repository.

Neither .beads/.gitignore nor the root .gitignore excludes this file. The file is not in scope for source control — it is a runtime credential analogous to .env secrets.

Fix: Remove this file from the PR, add '.beads-credential-key' to .beads/.gitignore, and rotate the key if it has been pushed to a remote.

title: .beads/.beads-credential-key committed to VCS — private credential file with mode 0600 should not be tracked
description:
File: .beads/.beads-credential-key

The PR adds .beads/.beads-credential-key to version control. This file:
- Has filesystem permissions 0600 (owner-read/write only), the standard marker for a credentials/key file
- Contains: seanb4t/specgraph
- Is NOT listed in .beads/.gitignore or the root .gitignore

The name '.beads-credential-key' and the 0600 permissions strongly indicate this is an authentication token or credential identifier used by the bd/beads toolchain to authenticate against the beads remote (specgraph/specgraph). Even if the current content is only a namespace string, the file is the designated location for credential material — committing it sets a pattern where a real token would also get committed.

Risk: If the beads remote uses this file as an auth token or lookup key, committing it to a public repo exposes credentials. Anyone who clones the repo and runs bd commands could authenticate as the repository owner.

Remediation:
1. Immediately remove this file from the commit and from git history if it has already been pushed.
2. Add .beads/.beads-credential-key to .beads/.gitignore or the root .gitignore.
3. If this file only holds a namespace identifier (not a secret), rename it to something without 'credential' in the name and document it as non-sensitive, then it may be safe to track.
close_reason: Already fixed: .beads-credential-key is untracked and listed in .beads/.gitignore

title: Investigate single-writer assumption and add transactional guarantees
description:
CodeRabbit flagged that lifecycle mutations + ChangeLog creation are not atomic — the spec mutation commits, then recomputeContentHash, GetSpec, and createChangeLog run as separate queries. If another writer interleaves, the ChangeLog could record wrong version/hash, or a partial write could leave a mutation without its checkpoint.

The codebase comments claim single-writer (e.g., UpdateSpec: 'Acceptable because SpecGraph is single-writer'), but this may not hold in all deployment scenarios (multiple CLI instances, daemon + CLI, future multi-agent).

**Investigate:**
1. Is SpecGraph actually single-writer today? (daemon mode, CLI concurrency, agent workers)
2. What breaks if it's not? (stale ChangeLog hashes, orphaned mutations without checkpoints, supersede with only one side's ChangeLog)
3. What are the options? (Memgraph transactions, single guarded Cypher queries, version guards on ChangeLog creation, advisory locks)

**Then address:**
- Add transactional guarantees or version guards to lifecycle + ChangeLog write paths
- Document the concurrency model explicitly
- Update or remove single-writer comments if the assumption is wrong
close_reason: Implemented: all 12 multi-query write paths wrapped in RunInTransaction. ADR-004 documents the optimistic concurrency model.

title: Amend re-entry lands AT target stage, blocking re-authoring from that stage
description:
## Bug

When amending a spec with `--re-entry shape`, the spec's stage is set TO `shape`. But the `shape` CLI command expects to transition FROM `spark` TO `shape` — so running `specgraph shape <slug> --json-file` after amend fails with `invalid stage transition` because it's `shape → shape` (a no-op).

This means amend with re-entry is broken for every stage: the spec lands at the target but can't re-author at that stage.

## Evidence

```
$ ./specgraph approve slack-authoring-integration
Approved: slack-authoring-integration at 2026-04-09T14:36:33Z

$ ./specgraph amend slack-authoring-integration --re-entry shape --reason 'Expanding scope'
Amended: slack-authoring-integration (stage=shape, lifecycle=SPEC_LIFECYCLE_TASK, version=10)

$ ./specgraph shape slack-authoring-integration --json-file /tmp/shape.json
shape: failed_precondition: invalid stage transition
```

The spec is at `shape` but the shape command tries `shape → shape` which ValidateTransition rejects as a no-op.

## Root cause

`LifecycleAmendSpec` in `internal/storage/postgres/lifecycle.go` sets `stage = targetStage` directly. The authoring commands (shape, specify, decompose) then try to transition FROM the current stage TO the same stage, which `ValidateTransition` in `internal/storage/stage_validation.go` rejects:

```go
// ValidateTransition line 51-53
if from == to {
    return fmt.Errorf("transition from %q to %q is a no-op", from, to)
}
```

## Desired behavior

Two possible fixes:

**Option A: Amend sets stage to one-before-target.** `--re-entry shape` sets stage to `spark`, so running `specgraph shape` does `spark → shape` which is valid. This means the re-entry stage is where you're going, not where you land.

**Option B: Authoring commands allow same-stage overwrite.** The shape/specify/decompose commands detect the spec is already at that stage (from an amend) and overwrite the output without a stage transition. This is more flexible but changes the transition model.

Option A is simpler and matches the user's mental model: '--re-entry shape' means 'I want to redo shape', which requires being at the stage before shape.

## Files involved

- `internal/storage/postgres/lifecycle.go:56` — sets stage to targetStage directly
- `internal/storage/stage_validation.go:50-53` — rejects same-to-same transitions
- `internal/storage/postgres/authoring.go` — StoreShapeOutput, StoreSpecifyOutput, StoreDecomposeOutput all call TransitionStage
- `cmd/specgraph/lifecycle.go` — CLI amend command
- Introduced by PR #889 (lifecycle nomenclature inversion)

title: Lifecycle nomenclature inversion: amend and supersede have swapped semantics
description:
The current lifecycle model has amend only working from 'done' (post-implementation) and supersede working from any non-terminal stage. This is inverted from natural semantics:

**Current (broken):**
- amend: only from done. 'I built this, it's wrong, send it back.'
- supersede: from any non-terminal. 'Replace with something new.'

**Proposed (natural):**
- amend: from >= approved && < done (in-flight work). 'We're still working on this but the spec needs to change - send it back to authoring.' You amend a bill before it's passed, not after.
- supersede: only from done. 'This finished work is obsolete and needs a fundamentally different replacement.'

**Evidence from docs:**
- site/docs/concepts/lifecycle.md says amend is 'post-completion' but the 'when to use' examples (scope refinement, misunderstood requirements) are things discovered during implementation, not after.
- The decision tree (lifecycle.md:120) starts with 'Spec reached done or needs to change' - the 'or' is ambiguous.
- No path exists from approved back to authoring except supersede, which is heavy-handed for pre-implementation scope tweaks.

**Files involved:**
- internal/storage/postgres/lifecycle.go - LifecycleAmendSpec SQL WHERE clause (line 56, hardcoded to done)
- internal/storage/lifecycle.go - LifecycleBackend interface comment
- internal/storage/spec_domain.go - SpecStage definitions
- internal/storage/stage_validation.go - ValidateAmendTransition
- internal/server/lifecycle_handler.go - TransitionAmend handler + lifecycleError
- site/docs/concepts/lifecycle.md - full lifecycle docs
- site/docs/concepts/authoring.md - authoring funnel state diagram (line 210: done-->amended)
- proto/specgraph/v1/lifecycle.proto - TransitionAmendRequest (no stage constraint in proto)

**Scope of change:**
- Amend: change eligible source stages from {done} to {approved, in_progress, review}
- Supersede: change to only work from done (currently works from any non-terminal)
- Update docs, tests, CLI help text, error messages
- Consider: should amend from in_progress/review preserve execution state (claim, progress)?
- Consider: what happens to slices when amending from in_progress?
close_reason: Lifecycle nomenclature inversion complete. Amend from {approved,in_progress,review}, supersede from {done} only. Amended stage removed. Branch: feat/lifecycle-nomenclature-inversion

title: CreateSyncMapping populates SpecID with ContentHash instead of an actual ID
description:
In sync.go:53, the returned SyncMapping has:

  SpecID: spec.ContentHash, // no integer ID in postgres; use content hash as proxy

The storage.SyncMapping.SpecID field is documented as the spec's identifier. Populating it with the content hash (a murmur3-128 hex digest) will cause any consumer that uses SyncMapping.SpecID to look up a spec by ID to fail. The comment acknowledges this is a workaround but it is incorrect: the spec has a proper string ID (spec.ID from the SELECT). If the field is not meaningful in Postgres, it should be left empty ('') or set to spec.ID, not set to a value that looks like an ID but is semantically wrong.

File: internal/storage/postgres/sync.go, line 53.

Fix: change to SpecID: spec.ID or SpecID: '' depending on what consumers expect.

title: GetExecutionEvents LIMIT 0 returns zero rows instead of unlimited
description:
In execution.go:181, the query hard-codes LIMIT $3 and always passes the limit argument. PostgreSQL interprets LIMIT 0 as returning zero rows, not as unlimited. Any caller that passes limit=0 to signal 'no limit' will receive an empty result set silently. The correct pattern (used in ListSpecs at spec.go:258) is:

  LIMIT CASE WHEN $N > 0 THEN $N END

which evaluates to NULL (no limit) when the value is 0 or negative.

File: internal/storage/postgres/execution.go, line 181.

Fix: replace 'LIMIT $3' with 'LIMIT CASE WHEN $3 > 0 THEN $3 END'.

title: StoreFindings deletes all HAS_FINDING edges for a spec when updating one pass type
description:
In findings.go:41-47, StoreFindings first deletes findings filtered by pass_type (correct), but then deletes ALL HAS_FINDING edges for the spec regardless of pass_type:

  DELETE FROM edges WHERE from_slug = $1 AND edge_type = 'HAS_FINDING' AND project_slug = $2

This means storing findings for pass type A will silently delete the HAS_FINDING edges for pass type B's findings even though those finding rows are untouched. After the operation, ListFindings by ID will still return B's findings but they will be unreachable via the graph edge traversal, creating a split between the findings table and the edges table.

File: internal/storage/postgres/findings.go, lines 41-47.

Fix: add an IN (subquery) or a join to restrict the edge deletion to the specific pass_type being replaced:
  DELETE FROM edges
  WHERE from_slug = $1 AND edge_type = 'HAS_FINDING' AND project_slug = $2
    AND to_slug IN (SELECT id FROM findings WHERE spec_slug = $1 AND pass_type = $3 AND project_slug = $2)

title: UnclaimSpec deletes claim and edge non-atomically
description:
In claim.go:128-162, UnclaimSpec issues a SELECT to read the active agent, then two separate DELETE statements (DELETE FROM claims, DELETE FROM edges) with no wrapping transaction. Between the claim check and the deletes, another request could insert a new claim; more critically, if the process crashes after the first DELETE (claims) but before the second (edges), the CLAIMED_BY edge is left dangling permanently. LifecycleAcknowledgeDrift and RecordCompletion both use RunInTransaction for their equivalent multi-step writes.

File: internal/storage/postgres/claim.go, lines 128-162.

Fix: wrap the SELECT scan, DELETE FROM claims, and DELETE FROM edges in a single s.RunInTransaction call.

title: SupersedeSpec (authoring) is not atomic and skips version bump and changelog
description:
In authoring.go:292-324, SupersedeSpec performs two separate exec calls (UPDATE specs, then INSERT INTO edges) outside of any transaction. If the process crashes between these two statements, the spec will be marked superseded without the SUPERSEDES edge, leaving the graph in an inconsistent state. Additionally, unlike LifecycleSupersedeSpec, this authoring variant does not: (1) bump the spec version, (2) recompute the content hash, or (3) create a changelog entry. This means the spec's version counter is out of sync with its history and optimistic concurrency guards on subsequent operations can silently pass or fail incorrectly.

File: internal/storage/postgres/authoring.go, lines 292-324.

Fix: wrap the UPDATE and INSERT in s.RunInTransaction; add version = version + 1 to the UPDATE clause; call s.recomputeContentHash after the UPDATE; and call s.createChangeLog inside the transaction.

title: UpdateConstitution silently ignores error when looking up existing constitution ID
description: constitution.go:81-89 — When id is empty, UpdateConstitution queries for an existing constitution ID with: existErr := s.queryRow(...).Scan(&existingID); if existErr == nil && existingID != '' { id = existingID } else { id = newID("con") }. Any DB error (connection failure, timeout, etc.) is treated identically to 'no existing constitution found', resulting in a new ID being generated silently. This creates a second constitution row instead of updating the existing one, potentially leaving the project with multiple constitution rows and causing the ORDER BY version DESC LIMIT 1 in GetConstitution to return stale data. Fix: explicitly check errors.Is(existErr, pgx.ErrNoRows); return any other error.

title: ReleaseExpiredClaims deletes edges outside the claims-delete transaction
description: execution.go:250-287 — ReleaseExpiredClaims issues the DELETE FROM claims CTE as one query, collects the deleted rows, then issues individual DELETE FROM edges queries in a loop — all outside a transaction. If a process crashes between the claims delete and the edges delete, or if the edges delete fails partway through the loop (line 283: early return on first failure but partial deletes already committed), the claims table and edges table are left inconsistent: claims are gone but CLAIMED_BY edges remain, or some edges are deleted and others are not. Fix: wrap both the claims delete and all edge deletes in a single RunInTransaction call.

title: UnclaimSpec has TOCTOU race: claim check and delete are not in a transaction
description: claim.go:128-161 — UnclaimSpec reads the current agent with queryRow (outside any transaction) then performs a separate DELETE. Between the SELECT and the DELETE, another concurrent operation could change the claim state (e.g., the claim expires and is re-acquired by a different agent). The check that activeAgent == agent may pass on stale data, and the DELETE may then remove a different agent's valid claim. By contrast, ClaimSpec correctly wraps its read-modify-write in RunInTransaction. Fix: wrap the SELECT+DELETE in RunInTransaction, or combine into a single conditional DELETE that returns the deleted agent for validation.

title: recomputeContentHash silently drops json.Marshal errors for authoring outputs
description: lifecycle.go:448-466 and authoring.go:469-488 — Both recomputeContentHash and readSpecFields marshal authoring outputs (sparkOutput, shapeOutput, specifyOutput, decomposeOutput) with patterns like: if b, mErr := json.Marshal(sparkOutput); mErr == nil { outputs[...] = string(b) }. When json.Marshal fails (e.g., due to a type that cannot be marshaled), the error is silently discarded and the field is simply omitted from the hash inputs. This means the content hash is computed from an incomplete set of fields, producing a hash that diverges from the true stored content. Subsequent drift detection will see false-negative drift. Fix: propagate json.Marshal errors as function errors rather than silently omitting the field.

title: dispatchChangeEvents errors are silently discarded after commit
description: tx.go:62-63 — dispatchChangeEvents is called after a successful Commit with no error return and no way to signal failure to the caller. If OnSpecChanged fails (subscriber returns an error), that failure is completely invisible: the caller sees a nil error from RunInTransaction even though the notification did not reach the subscriber. Panics are caught and logged (good), but non-panic errors from OnSpecChanged are not surfaced at all — they are simply discarded because the subscriber interface appears to return nothing. If the subscriber signature returns an error, it should be logged at minimum. If downstream consumers depend on these events for consistency (e.g. an in-memory index), silent failures corrupt state without any signal to operators. Fix: at minimum log subscriber errors at slog.Error level, and consider a policy on whether subscriber errors should block or be fire-and-forget.

title: cli-cookbook.md recipe 3 calls report-progress on a slice slug, but report-progress is a spec-level command

site/docs/guides/cli-cookbook.md Recipe 3 (Work with slices) shows:

  specgraph report-progress auth-service.slice.1 --message "JWT signing logic done..."

Two problems:

2. The --agent flag is missing (it is required on report-progress per cmd/specgraph/report.go).

This creates confusion between the spec-level execution lifecycle (claim/report-progress/report-completion) and the slice-level lifecycle (slice claim/slice complete).

Suggested fix: Remove the report-progress call from Recipe 3. The slice workflow ends with 'slice complete'. If the intent is to show progress reporting, it belongs in Recipe 6 with spec-level lifecycle commands.

title: docs:cli:check task writes to shared /tmp path causing CI race conditions
description:
Taskfile.yml lines 266-267: the docs:cli:check task hardcodes /tmp/cli-reference-check.md as its scratch file. This path is shared across the filesystem — if CI runs multiple jobs or tasks concurrently (e.g., matrix builds), two runs will clobber each other's scratch file causing spurious diff failures or false passes.

Additionally, on macOS (the dev platform) the sandboxed /tmp may behave differently than expected.

Fix: Use a task-local temp file or the Task's built-in .task/ working directory:
  - ./{{.BINARY_NAME}} docs cli .task/cli-reference-check.md
  - diff -u site/docs/cli-reference.md .task/cli-reference-check.md

Or use mktemp if shell expansion is available in Taskfile:
  - TMP=$(mktemp) && ./{{.BINARY_NAME}} docs cli "$TMP" && diff -u site/docs/cli-reference.md "$TMP" && rm "$TMP"

File: Taskfile.yml lines 263-267

title: Anchor mismatch: TOC link #server-config won't resolve to heading ## Server & Config
description:
In cmd/specgraph/docs.go:86, writeTOC generates the anchor for 'Server & Config' by replacing ' & ' with '-', producing #server-config. But the corresponding section heading written by writeGroups is '## Server & Config'. Standard markdown renderers (GitHub, MkDocs) generate anchors from headings by lowercasing, stripping non-alphanumeric non-hyphen characters (including '&'), then replacing spaces with hyphens — producing 'server--config' (double hyphen). The TOC entry therefore points to #server-config which will not match the generated anchor #server--config, breaking navigation.

Fix: Change the anchor generation in writeTOC to match how the renderer processes the heading text. Either strip '&' and preserve the surrounding spaces before lowercasing/hyphenating, or sanitize the heading string through the same pipeline used by the renderer. Example fix:
  anchor := strings.ToLower(g.Name)
  anchor = strings.NewReplacer("&", "", "  ", " ").Replace(anchor)  // strip & and collapse double-space
  anchor = strings.ReplaceAll(anchor, " ", "-")
  anchor = strings.Trim(anchor, "-")

File: cmd/specgraph/docs.go lines 83-88
Test coverage: TestWriteTOC only checks 'Drift & Linting' (which becomes #drift-linting via direct ' & '->'-' replacement and works correctly), but does not cover 'Server & Config', masking this bug.

title: Execution bundle output is not agent-actionable
description:
The current execution bundle (GenerateBundle RPC + renderBundleYAML) is too thin for an agent to act on. It only includes: version, spec slug/intent/stage, decision slug/title/status, and callback endpoint. An agent receiving this has no orientation on what to build or how to report back.

**Design principle**: The bundle should be a launchpad — enough inline context to orient the agent, plus pointers to get fresh data. NOT a full data dump (stale data risk) and NOT so opaque it requires a skill to interpret.

**What's missing (inline context)**:
- Acceptance criteria / key requirements from specify output (the 'what')
- Subtask list from decompose output (the 'how to chunk work')
- Key constraints from constitution (the 'guardrails')
- Brief dependency context (what upstream specs exist and their status)

**What's missing (pointers)**:
- GetPrime endpoint with clear instructions on when/how to call it
- How to use ReportProgress/Blocker/Completion with concrete examples
- Where to find full spec detail if the agent needs to drill deeper

**What to avoid**:
- Shipping full specify/decompose output inline (goes stale, bloats bundle)
- Requiring a dedicated skill just to parse the bundle
- Flat semicolon-joined strings for structured data (current PrimeResponse problem)
close_reason: Execution bundle redesigned as markdown launchpad with full authoring outputs, claim instructions, dependency drift state, and decision rationale

title: os.Chdir in TestRunConstitutionEmit_HappyPath_File mutates process-global state
description:
File: cmd/specgraph/constitution_test.go, TestRunConstitutionEmit_HappyPath_File (diff lines 722-748).

The test calls os.Chdir(dir) to change the process working directory, then restores it in t.Cleanup. os.Chdir is process-global — there is no per-goroutine working directory in Go. If the test binary runs with -parallel > 1 (the default for go test), another goroutine that reads or writes a relative path concurrently will use the wrong directory during the window between the Chdir and the cleanup. This is a latent race condition that will cause non-deterministic failures as coverage and the test suite grow.

Alternative: pass an absolute output path in emitOutput rather than changing the working directory. Since the test already resolves emitOutput = "out.md" as relative-to-cwd, either:

  1. Set emitOutput to filepath.Join(dir, "out.md") so no Chdir is needed, if runConstitutionEmit accepts an absolute path (preferred), or
  2. If the production code insists on a relative path, mark the test with t.Setenv or restructure to avoid mutating global state.

The t.Cleanup restoration is not sufficient protection for concurrent tests.

title: os.Chdir in TestRunConstitutionEmit_HappyPath_File is a test isolation hazard
description:
cmd/specgraph/constitution_test.go:738 — TestRunConstitutionEmit_HappyPath_File calls os.Chdir to change the working directory of the test process, which is a package-level side-effect. If this test runs concurrently with another test that relies on the working directory (e.g. runInit, runPrime, or the existing init_test.go), both tests may see an unexpected cwd.

While no tests currently declare t.Parallel(), this is a latent flakiness risk: if a future contributor adds parallel tests, or if the test binary is run with -parallel N > 1, the os.Chdir will race.

The fix is to restructure runConstitutionEmit to accept an optional cwd override (injected for tests) or use an os.DirFS-based approach, rather than a global chdir. Alternatively, a package-level serialization mechanism (sync.Mutex) can protect the chdir call, but that's more invasive.

Also note: init_test.go already uses os.Chdir with the same pattern, so this is a pre-existing problem, but the PR adds a second instance.

File: cmd/specgraph/constitution_test.go:727-747

title: Hardcoded /tmp path in TestRunConversationRecord_MissingFile makes test unreliable
description:
File: cmd/specgraph/conversation_test.go, in TestRunConversationRecord_MissingFile (line ~943 in diff).

The test sets convRecordJSONFile = "/tmp/does-not-exist-conv.json" to exercise the missing-file error branch. If this file happens to exist on the test machine (e.g., left over from a previous test run, or created by another process), the function will not return a missing-file error. Instead it will attempt to parse it, possibly succeeding or returning a different error — causing the test to either fail or silently verify the wrong branch.

Every other missing-file test in the PR uses t.TempDir() to get a guaranteed-clean directory. Fix:

  setConvRecordVars(t, filepath.Join(t.TempDir(), "does-not-exist-conv.json"), false)

This is consistent with the pattern used in, e.g., TestRunShape_MissingJSONFile and TestLoadJSONFileRaw_NonexistentPath in the same PR.

title: runStatus: 'not running' (network error) branch not exercised by any test
description:
cmd/specgraph/status.go:58-74 — runStatus has a branch that handles connection/timeout errors by printing 'not running' rather than returning an error. This is the normal user-facing path when the server is stopped, and it has distinct behavior (returns nil, prints 'not running' to stdout). None of the new status tests cover this path.

The existing TestRunStatus_RPCError exercises CodePermissionDenied which propagates as a real error — that is the non-connection path. But the net.Error / DeadlineExceeded path (the most common real-world case) is untested.

Suggested fix: Start a server that immediately closes connections (or use a port with nothing listening after briefly pointing config at a closed port) and assert: require.NoError(t, err) with some output check for 'not running'.

title: runConstitutionEmit: path traversal guard branch has no test
description:

This branch is not tested in the PR. A test should supply an emitOutput path like "../../etc/shadow" (relative) or an absolute path outside the tempdir and assert the error contains "outside current directory". Without this test, a regression that accidentally removes the guard would go undetected.

Suggested test name: TestRunConstitutionEmit_OutputOutsideCwd

title: TestRunConstitutionEmit_HappyPath_File uses os.Chdir without t.Parallel guard — can corrupt other tests
description: In cmd/specgraph/constitution_test.go (TestRunConstitutionEmit_HappyPath_File, lines 722-748), the test calls os.Chdir(dir) to simulate runConstitutionEmit's cwd-relative path check. The cleanup restores the original dir via t.Cleanup, but Go's testing package runs tests in the same package sequentially by default — however if any test in this package is later marked t.Parallel(), or if the test binary is run with -parallel > 1, the Chdir will affect concurrent tests that rely on the working directory. Additionally, the test silently ignores the error from the final os.Chdir(origDir) in the cleanup: '_ = os.Chdir(origDir)'. If restoring the original directory fails (e.g. the original dir was deleted), subsequent tests run in a wrong directory and can fail with confusing errors, with no diagnostic that the cleanup itself failed. Fix: use require.NoError(t, os.Chdir(origDir)) in the cleanup, or better, restructure the test to avoid Chdir entirely by passing an absolute path.

title: TestReadyCmd_AcceptsNoArgs is a no-op when readyCmd.Args is nil
description: In cmd/specgraph/graph_test.go (TestReadyCmd_AcceptsNoArgs), the test body is guarded by 'if readyCmd.Args \!= nil'. When readyCmd.Args is nil (which is the expected state the comment describes), the entire test body is skipped and the test passes trivially without asserting anything. This is a silent false-pass: the test is supposed to document that readyCmd accepts no args, but it never calls require.NoError or any assertion. The correct pattern is assert.Nil(t, readyCmd.Args, ...) — exactly as TestListCmd_AcceptsNoArgs does it in spec_test.go. Fix: replace the if-guard + require.NoError block with assert.Nil(t, readyCmd.Args, "readyCmd should not restrict args").

title: Approve skill should not let the authoring agent self-approve a spec
description: After decompose, the agent asks 'One final stage: Approve. Want to review and sign off on this spec?' — effectively offering to self-review and self-approve work it just authored. The approve stage requires human judgment (checking spec against constitution, verifying completeness, assessing risk). The agent that authored the spec through spark→shape→specify→decompose should not be the one approving it. The approve skill needs guardrails: (1) it should always require explicit human confirmation, not just 'yes' from the same conversation flow, (2) it should present the full checklist and wait for human sign-off on each point, (3) ideally the approval should come from a different session/agent than the one that authored the stages. The current skill posture may be too eager — 'Want to review and sign off?' implies the agent will do the review, not that the human should.

title: specgraph spark CLI: exit 1 with usage dump instead of error message
description: When 'specgraph spark <slug> --seed ...' fails, it prints the cobra usage help and exits 1 without showing the actual error message. This happens because SilenceUsage is not set on the spark command — cobra defaults to printing usage on any RunE error. The real error (likely an RPC error like 'spec already has spark output' or a stage transition failure) is hidden behind the usage dump. Seen in a session where 'specgraph create' succeeded, then 'specgraph spark' on the same slug failed twice with usage-only output, while 'specgraph show' confirmed the spec exists. Fix: set SilenceUsage: true on sparkCmd (and audit all other commands for the same issue).

title: No test for cross-project slug isolation: same slug in different projects should succeed
description:
The uniqueness check query MATCH (p:Project {slug: $project})<-[:BELONGS_TO]-(s:Spec {slug: $slug}) scopes uniqueness to a project, meaning the same slug in two different projects should be allowed. No test in memgraph_test.go verifies this boundary.

Without this test, a future regression that accidentally enforces global slug uniqueness (e.g., dropping the project filter from the Cypher query) would go undetected.

Suggested test in internal/storage/memgraph/memgraph_test.go:
- Create store1 with WithProject("proj-a"), create store2 with WithProject("proj-b")
- Call store1.CreateSpec(ctx, "shared-slug", ...)
- Call store2.CreateSpec(ctx, "shared-slug", ...) — must succeed (no error)

Criticality: 7 — if the query is ever widened to global scope, multi-tenant data is silently broken.
close_reason: Fixed: cross-project test, unique slugs in auth, e2e duplicate slug test, priority naming

title: ErrSpecAlreadyExists passes raw error chain to client, leaking internal details
close_reason: Fixed: slug_key constraint + error sanitization + existsParams reuse

title: ClearAll in BeforeEach wipes entire shared Memgraph DB before each auth test
description:
File: e2e/api/auth_test.go, in the BeforeEach of 'keys configured, valid admin key'.

The PR adds Expect(info.Store.ClearAll(ctx)).To(Succeed()) before seeding 'admin-test-spec'. ClearAll issues MATCH (n) DETACH DELETE n, which deletes every node and relationship in the shared Memgraph instance (identified by memgraphBoltURI). All test suites in the api e2e package share this single Memgraph instance.

The immediate motivation is valid: with slug uniqueness now enforced, re-seeding the same slug across BeforeEach invocations would fail. But ClearAll is disproportionate — it nukes data from all other Describe blocks (pipeline, authoring, constitution, claims, etc.), not just the auth test's own seed.

Currently this is contained because all other stateful suites use Ordered containers, which Ginkgo 2 guarantees run consecutively without interleaving. However:
1. The approach is fragile — adding any non-Ordered suite that relies on pre-existing data will silently break when auth BeforeEach fires.
2. There is no AfterEach that restores state, so auth tests leave a partially-cleared DB (only e2e-test project node) for anything that runs after.

Suggested fix: Instead of ClearAll, use a unique slug per test invocation (e.g., append a random suffix or use Ginkgo's CurrentSpecReport().FullText() hash) or add a targeted delete of the specific seeded slug before re-creating it. Alternatively, add Expect(info.Store.ClearAll(ctx)).To(Succeed()) to AfterEach instead, so cleanup is scoped to after each auth test completes rather than before.
close_reason: Fixed: cross-project test, unique slugs in auth, e2e duplicate slug test, priority naming

title: TOCTOU race: check-then-create lacks DB-level unique constraint, allowing duplicate slugs under concurrency
description:
The PR adds a read-then-create duplicate check inside a Memgraph write transaction (`RunInTransaction`), but there is no database-level UNIQUE constraint on `(Spec.slug, Project)`. Only a non-unique performance index exists (`CREATE INDEX ON :Spec(slug)`).

**File/location:** `internal/storage/memgraph/memgraph.go` lines 191-203

**Problem:** Memgraph uses optimistic concurrency (snapshot isolation). Two concurrent `CreateSpec` requests with the same slug can both execute the existence check query, both observe zero results, and both proceed to CREATE — with no DB-level enforcement to reject the second writer. The transaction conflict detection (`ErrConcurrentModification`) only fires on conflicting writes to the *same node/edge*, not on write-after-read races against non-existent nodes.

**Impact:** The uniqueness guarantee is not atomic. Under concurrent load (e.g., agent retries, parallel client calls) duplicate spec slugs within a project can be created silently, bypassing the new check. This is an integrity violation that could also be exploited deliberately by racing requests.

**Suggested fix:** Add a database-level uniqueness constraint:
```cypher
CREATE CONSTRAINT ON (s:Spec) ASSERT s.slug IS UNIQUE
```
or a composite constraint if Memgraph supports it for the project-scoped case. Alternatively, replace the `MATCH + CREATE` pattern with a `MERGE ... ON CREATE SET ... ON MATCH SET s.__dup = true` idiom that lets the DB enforce atomicity, then inspect the result to distinguish create vs. collision. The existing index already provides the lookup performance; adding the uniqueness enforcement closes the race.
close_reason: Fixed: slug_key constraint + error sanitization + existsParams reuse

title: CreateSpec allows duplicate slugs - no uniqueness enforcement
description: Two specs with slug 'webhook-notifications' exist in the demo database with different IDs (spec-01KM9C24C0KRWQRZBK3G8QW926 and spec-01KM9C2AX1HMXG978ZSSKADEB2), both at approved stage. Root cause: slug uniqueness is not enforced at any level. (1) No UNIQUE constraint on Spec.slug in Memgraph schema -- only a regular index. (2) CreateSpec in memgraph.go does a blind CREATE without checking for existing slugs. (3) ErrSpecAlreadyExists sentinel exists in storage/authoring.go but is never returned. (4) GetSpec returns records[0] silently hiding duplicates, while ListSpecs returns all -- inconsistent. Fix: Add UNIQUE constraint on (project, slug) pair in Memgraph, add pre-check in CreateSpec that returns ErrSpecAlreadyExists, map to CodeAlreadyExists in the handler. This is a potential demo showstopper since it creates ghost specs that receive funnel stage data independently.

title: Keep skill format references in sync with proto schema changes
description: The plugin skills (shape, specify, decompose) now have references/*-output-format.md files that document the expected JSON schema for each stage's CLI command. These schemas mirror the proto messages (ShapeOutput, SpecifyOutput, DecomposeOutput). When proto fields change, these reference docs must be updated too. Consider: (1) adding a CLAUDE.md gotcha about this, (2) CI validation that compares the reference docs against the proto definitions (e.g., a script that extracts field names from proto and checks they appear in the reference docs), (3) or generating the reference docs from proto as part of 'task proto'.
notes: Confirmed breaking: shape skill generated JSON with object-type risks/tradeoffs and recommended field on Approach, but proto ShapeOutput uses repeated string for risks/tradeoffs and has no recommended field. This caused specgraph shape --json-file to fail with exit 1 during a demo session (screenshot evidence).
close_reason: PR #670 — skill format references fixed to match proto (camelCase, correct types, removed nonexistent fields)

title: Shape skill: background scan result treated as scope approval
description: When the shape skill dispatches a background codebase scan and then presents scope in/out, it should wait for explicit user approval before moving to approaches. Currently, when the background scan agent returns results, the skill interprets the agent notification as user input and skips the scope approval step. The skill says 'You approved the scope' when the user never actually responded. Fix: the skill should explicitly gate on user confirmation for each section (scope, approaches, decisions, risks, success criteria) regardless of background agent notifications.

title: specgraph up: resolve absolute binary path for LaunchAgent plist
description: When specgraph up installs a LaunchAgent on macOS, os.Executable() may return a relative path (e.g., ./specgraph) which launchctl rejects or can't find after the working directory changes. The service.Generate function should resolve the binary path to an absolute path (filepath.Abs or exec.LookPath) before writing it into the plist. This caused the demo bootstrap to fail with a launchctl service install error, requiring a fallback to specgraph serve.
close_reason: Root cause: stale service label from previous e2e run with temp binary path. Fix: bootout existing service before bootstrap in install().

title: StoreSafetyFlags silently moved from PassWriter into StageWriter breaking interface composition
description:
internal/storage/authoring.go: StoreSafetyFlags was moved from the deleted PassWriter interface directly into StageWriter. This is a behavioral interface change that may be invisible to readers but has contract implications:

Before:
  type StageWriter interface { TransitionStage, StoreSparkOutput, StoreShapeOutput, StoreSpecifyOutput, StoreDecomposeOutput }
  type PassWriter interface { StoreRedTeamFindings, StorePeripheralVision, StoreConsistencyIssues, StoreSimplicityFindings, StoreSafetyFlags, StoreConstitutionViolations }
  type AuthoringBackend = StageWriter + PassWriter + AuthoringSpecLifecycle

After:
  type StageWriter interface { TransitionStage, StoreSparkOutput, ..., StoreDecomposeOutput, StoreSafetyFlags }  ← safety flags moved here
  type AuthoringBackend = StageWriter + AuthoringSpecLifecycle  ← PassWriter removed entirely

The design doc says safety flags are produced by deterministic in-process checks and run inline during stage transitions, so moving StoreSafetyFlags to StageWriter is intentional. However:

1. The CLAUDE.md note says 'StoreSafetyFlags moves from PassWriter to StageWriter (it runs inline during stage transitions, which is StageWriter's domain)' — this is documented in the design spec but NOT in any changelog or comment in authoring.go itself.
2. Any external code that satisfies the old AuthoringBackend interface via PassWriter will fail to compile — it now needs StoreSafetyFlags in the StageWriter position (same method, different composing interface).

Since the project is pre-1.0 internal-only this is acceptable risk, but the interface boundary change should be documented. The design spec document exists (docs/superpowers/specs/2026-03-20-analytical-pass-system-design.md) but a comment on StageWriter explaining why StoreSafetyFlags is there would prevent future confusion.

**Suggested fix**: Add a brief comment on StoreSafetyFlags in StageWriter noting it runs inline during stage transitions (not as a separate pass).
close_reason: Fixed directly — added comments, reserved declarations, and enforced passType invariant

title: constitution_check.md show_dep tool description omits {slug} substitution instruction
description:
File: internal/server/templates/constitution_check.md, line 20 (Available Information table).

The 'show_dep' row in the template's tool table reads:
  | show_dep | Full content of a specific dependency (for cross-spec context) |

The actual ToolReference returned by passToolManifest() (analytical_pass_handler.go) has:
  Description: "Read a specific dependency's full content (replace {slug} with the dependency slug)"

The template omits the critical instruction that the LLM must substitute '{slug}' in the command before invoking the tool. The LLM reading the template as a system prompt will see 'specgraph show {slug} --json' in the tool manifest but has no guidance in the template table that it needs to fill that placeholder. This creates a disconnect between the template documentation and actual usage.

Suggested fix: Update the show_dep table row to match the ToolReference description:
  | show_dep | Full content of a specific dependency (replace {slug} with the dependency slug) |

title: findingSeverityToProto silently maps corrupt/unknown severity to UNSPECIFIED, masking data integrity errors
description:
internal/server/analytical_pass_handler.go findingSeverityToProto (lines 207-218) has a default case that returns FINDING_SEVERITY_UNSPECIFIED for any unknown severity string:

  default:
      return specv1.FindingSeverity_FINDING_SEVERITY_UNSPECIFIED

If a Finding node in Memgraph carries a corrupted, migrated, or unrecognized severity string (e.g., from a future schema change or a data bug), the error is silently swallowed and the finding is returned to the caller with severity UNSPECIFIED. The caller (an agent) cannot distinguish a genuinely unspecified severity from a data integrity problem.

Contrast with findingSeverityFromProto (lines 193-203), which correctly returns an error for unknown values on the inbound path.

The outbound path should be symmetric: return an error (or log with slog.Error and return CodeInternal) when an unrecognized severity is encountered rather than silently replacing it with UNSPECIFIED. At minimum the corrupt value should be logged so operators can detect schema drift.

Suggested fix in ListFindings loop:
  protoSev := findingSeverityToProto(f.Severity)
  if protoSev == specv1.FindingSeverity_FINDING_SEVERITY_UNSPECIFIED {
      h.logger.Error("unknown finding severity from storage", "severity", f.Severity, "id", f.ID)
      return nil, connect.NewError(connect.CodeInternal, fmt.Errorf("finding %q has unrecognized severity %q", f.ID, f.Severity))
  }

title: AnalyticalFinding.PassType is redundant with StoreFindings passType param — invariant not enforced
description:
internal/storage/findings.go: FindingsWriter.StoreFindings takes both a top-level passType parameter AND accepts []AnalyticalFinding where each element has a PassType field. This creates an unenforceable invariant: callers can pass findings whose element PassType differs from the method-level passType.

The current memgraph implementation (findings.go line ~2292) uses f.PassType from the element for the node property, not the method-level param — which means the two sources can silently diverge.

Two design alternatives are cleaner:
1. Remove PassType from AnalyticalFinding (the struct used for input) — it's always derived from the method context. The method-level passType is the single source of truth. PassType on the returned struct from ListFindings is fine (read-side).
2. Or: have the memgraph implementation enforce the invariant by always using the method-level passType and ignoring element.PassType on write.

The deeper issue is that AnalyticalFinding serves dual purpose as both input and output, which makes the PassType field semantically ambiguous: required on read (it's set by storage), unnecessary/dangerous on write (it should be derived from context). Consider splitting into an input type (no PassType, no ID, no CreatedAt, no Version) and a read type — analogous to the proto split of AnalyticalFindingInput vs AnalyticalFinding.

title: Proto field removals lack reserved declarations per project standard
description:
CLAUDE.md states: 'Proto field removal — When removing a proto field, use reserved for both field number and name in the .proto file.' This PR removes multiple fields across three proto files without adding any reserved declarations:

**proto/specgraph/v1/authoring.proto**
- SparkResponse: field 3 (constitution_violations / ConstitutionViolation)
- ShapeResponse: fields 2 (peripheral_vision / PeripheralVisionItem) and 4 (constitution_violations / ConstitutionViolation)
- SpecifyResponse: fields 2 (red_team / RedTeamFinding), 3 (consistency_issues / ConsistencyIssue), 5 (constitution_violations / ConstitutionViolation)
- DecomposeResponse: fields 2 (simplicity / SimplicityFinding), 4 (constitution_violations / ConstitutionViolation)

**proto/specgraph/v1/constitution.proto**
- ConstitutionService.CheckViolation RPC removed without any comment/reserved on the service
- Violation message removed (fields 1-4), ViolationSeverity enum removed

The design doc explicitly overrides this: 'Clean break — no reserved needed. Pre-1.0, no external consumers.' This creates a direct conflict between the PR's documented intent and the codified project standard in CLAUDE.md.

**Suggested fix**: Either (a) add reserved declarations per CLAUDE.md to close the gap between the standard and this PR, or (b) update CLAUDE.md to document the pre-1.0 exception. Without reserved markers, field numbers 2-5 on those response messages are silently reusable, which could cause silent deserialization corruption if any future field reuses those numbers with a different type.
close_reason: Fixed directly — added comments, reserved declarations, and enforced passType invariant

title: AnalyticalPassHandler.logger field declared and initialized but never used
description:
internal/server/analytical_pass_handler.go: The AnalyticalPassHandler struct declares a logger *slog.Logger field (line ~737) and it is set in RegisterAnalyticalPassService via logger: slog.Default() (line ~747), but h.logger is never referenced in any of the three handler methods (RunAnalyticalPass, StoreFindings, ListFindings).

This will be caught by golangci-lint (unused struct field) and will cause task check / the pre-push hook to fail. All other handlers in the codebase (e.g. ConstitutionHandler, AuthoringHandler) either use their logger field or do not declare one.

Fix: Either remove the logger field and initialization, or add structured log calls (e.g., h.logger.InfoContext / h.logger.WarnContext) consistent with how other handlers in this codebase use it.

title: passToolManifest embeds caller-controlled slug directly into CLI command strings without sanitization
description: internal/server/analytical_pass_handler.go, passToolManifest function (lines ~892-899): the slug parameter received from the RPC caller is interpolated directly into the Command field of ToolReference entries using fmt.Sprintf, e.g. fmt.Sprintf("specgraph show %s --json", slug) and fmt.Sprintf("specgraph edges %s --type DEPENDS_ON --json", slug). Although slug is validated by validateSlug before reaching this code, the ToolReference.Command field is returned to the calling agent as a string it will then execute. If validateSlug permits characters that have shell significance (semicolons, backticks, dollar signs, etc.), or if the agent passes this string through a shell interpreter rather than using exec-style invocation, the result is command injection. The risk is amplified because these tool commands are returned in a prompt template to an LLM agent that may invoke them via a shell. Fix: (1) Confirm validateSlug rejects all shell metacharacters; (2) explicitly quote the slug in each Command string, e.g. fmt.Sprintf("specgraph show %q --json", slug); (3) document in the ToolReference proto that Command values must not be passed through a shell without quoting.

title: StoreFindings handler: spec-not-found and null finding element paths untested
description: internal/server/analytical_pass_handler.go StoreFindings has two error branches without unit test coverage: (1) storage returns ErrSpecNotFound (lines ~831-833) → should map to CodeNotFound, and (2) a nil element in the findings slice (lines ~813-816) → should map to CodeInvalidArgument. The integration tests in findings_test.go cover the storage-level SpecNotFound but not the handler-level code path, and the nil element guard has no coverage at any level. Add TestStoreFindings_SpecNotFound and TestStoreFindings_NilFindingElement to analytical_pass_handler_test.go.
close_reason: Fixed — handler tests added, proto enum, CI hardening, length limits

title: ListFindings storage impl returns empty for nonexistent spec instead of ErrSpecNotFound
description:
internal/storage/memgraph/findings.go ListFindings does not verify the spec exists before querying. If the spec does not exist, the MATCH query finds no Spec node and returns zero rows — silently returning an empty slice instead of storage.ErrSpecNotFound.

This is inconsistent with the rest of the storage layer (GetSpec, StoreFindings all return ErrSpecNotFound for unknown slugs) and inconsistent with the handler's error-mapping code, which checks errors.Is(err, storage.ErrSpecNotFound) and maps it to CodeNotFound. The handler test TestListFindings_EmptyResult only exercises a spec-exists-but-has-no-findings case; there is no test for listing findings on a nonexistent spec.

Contrast with StoreFindings which calls s.GetSpec(txCtx, slug) inside the transaction and propagates ErrSpecNotFound.

Fix: Add a spec-existence check at the start of ListFindings (matching the StoreFindings pattern), or add a MATCH on the Spec node in the Cypher query with a count check, returning ErrSpecNotFound when the spec node is not found.

title: ListFindings silently returns empty slice for nonexistent spec — ErrSpecNotFound branch in handler is dead code
close_reason: Duplicate of spgr-bp7.9 (same ListFindings spec-not-found finding)

title: RunAnalyticalPass handler: empty slug not tested, CodeInvalidArgument path uncovered
description: internal/server/analytical_pass_handler.go RunAnalyticalPass validates slug via validateSlug() (line ~761) and pass_name emptiness (line ~763) before the pass type switch. Neither case is covered by the six new handler unit tests. The missing tests are: (1) empty slug returns CodeInvalidArgument, (2) empty pass_name (distinct from unknown pass_name) returns CodeInvalidArgument. TestRunAnalyticalPass_UnknownPassType covers an unknown pass name but not a blank string. Suggest adding TestRunAnalyticalPass_EmptySlug and TestRunAnalyticalPass_EmptyPassName to analytical_pass_handler_test.go.
close_reason: Fixed — handler tests added, proto enum, CI hardening, length limits

title: nscloud-cache-action and nscloud-setup-buildx-action pinned to floating version tags, not commit SHAs
description: The PR introduces three new third-party GitHub Actions references using floating mutable tags instead of pinned commit SHAs: namespacelabs/nscloud-cache-action@v1 (used in ci.yml and release.yml), namespacelabs/nscloud-setup-buildx-action@v0 (used in release.yml). Floating tags (v1, v0) can be updated by the action author at any time, making the tag point to different — potentially malicious — code. This is a supply chain risk. The existing actions in the same workflows (e.g. sigstore/cosign-installer@v4.1.0, anchore/sbom-action/download-syft@v0) have the same issue, but those are pre-existing. The new additions in this PR should be pinned to full commit SHAs. Fix: replace @v1 and @v0 with the current commit SHA for each action, following the same pattern used by other security-sensitive actions in GitHub's hardening guidelines. Example: namespacelabs/nscloud-cache-action@<full-sha>.
close_reason: Already fixed — all nscloud actions pinned to full commit SHAs: nscloud-cache-action@a90bb5d4 (v1.4.2), nscloud-setup-buildx-action@d059ed71 (v0.0.23)

title: Review: PR #571 — feat: analytical pass system with unified findings
description: PR review for analytical pass system. Replaces placeholder runAnalyticalPasses with agent-driven LLM architecture, unified AnalyticalFinding type, new AnalyticalPassService RPCs, embedded prompt templates. Also includes CI migration to Namespace runners.

title: quickstart.md inverts the init/up order required by spec
description: The spec (Section 3, Start the Server) defines the flow as: specgraph init my-project, then specgraph up. The quickstart.md implementation places the 'Start the Server' section (specgraph up only) before the 'Initialize a Project' section (specgraph init). This reverses the order prescribed by the spec: a user following the quickstart would run 'up' before 'init', which may fail or produce a confusing experience if up requires a .specgraph.yaml to exist. The spec plan doc also lists init before up in its code block.
close_reason: Fixed init/up order — init now comes before server start

title: quickstart.md advertises wrong default server port (7600 vs spec's 9090)
description: The spec (Section 3, Start the Server) states the server listens on localhost:9090 and registers ConnectRPC services. The quickstart.md implementation (line ~1839 in diff) states 'The server listens at http://localhost:7600 by default.' This contradicts the spec and also contradicts the spec's plan doc (which explicitly says port 9090). If 7600 is the real default, the spec is itself wrong and should be noted, but the discrepancy needs resolution before release. File: site/docs/quickstart.md.
close_reason: Already fixed: quickstart.md now correctly shows port 9090

title: quickstart.md omits Binary and Docker install paths required by spec
description: The spec (Section 2, Install) requires three install paths: Homebrew, Binary (download from GitHub releases, verify checksum, add to PATH), and Docker (docker pull ghcr.io/specgraph/specgraph:latest). The implementation in site/docs/quickstart.md only provides Homebrew and 'build from source'. The Binary download path and the Docker pull path are both missing. The spec explicitly notes that the Docker and Homebrew paths have a bootstrapping dependency on the first release existing, and says the quickstart should document this — the implementation handles this correctly for Homebrew via a comment but never mentions the binary or Docker paths at all.
close_reason: Added Binary and Docker install paths

title: quickstart.md: config syntax uses TOML notation for a YAML file
description:
site/docs/quickstart.md manual mode section (diff line 1843-1845) instructs users to set `server.mode = "manual"` in `~/.config/specgraph/config.yaml`. This uses TOML dot-notation with = assignment, but the config file format is YAML (internal/config/global.go uses gopkg.in/yaml.v3).

The correct YAML syntax is:
```yaml
server:
  mode: manual
```

A user following this instruction will write syntactically invalid YAML and the server will fail to load the config.
close_reason: Config syntax is correct — ~/.config/specgraph/config.yaml is YAML

title: passes.md: safety warning pattern list drops injection, eval(, exec( which are implemented

title: ecosystem.md says '10 skills' but plugin.json defines 11 skills
description:

The PR changes the Claude Code entry to: '10 skills and hooks integrate SpecGraph into the IDE workflow.'

title: .envrc committed despite being in .gitignore — exposes BD_ACTOR identity and BD_DOLT_AUTO_COMMIT setting
description:
File: .envrc

The PR commits .envrc which is explicitly listed in .gitignore (line 28). This is a gitignore violation and a security concern.

Contents committed:
  export BD_ACTOR=seanb4t
  export BD_DOLT_AUTO_COMMIT=on

Why this matters:
- .gitignore entry for .envrc exists precisely because this file is considered local/machine-specific and should not be shared. Committing it overrides that protection via a force-add.
- BD_ACTOR encodes a username that links the repo to a specific account identity on the beads remote. In a public repo this is not a secret, but the .gitignore intent signals it is not meant to be tracked.
- BD_DOLT_AUTO_COMMIT=on is a behavioral setting that will affect all clones of this repo that source .envrc via direnv, silently enabling auto-commit behavior for every contributor. This is an unintended side-effect configuration leak.

Remediation:
1. Remove .envrc from the commit (git rm --cached .envrc).
2. If a project-level default is needed, use a .envrc.example file (not ignored) and document it. Never commit the actual .envrc.

title: Decision: Stable ULID IDs with Murmur3-128 content hash
description:
## Decision

Keep stable ULID-based IDs for graph node identity. Add a separate content_hash field (Murmur3-128) for change detection on specs and decisions.

## Rationale

The docs described spec IDs as "content-addressable" but the implementation uses ULIDs (timestamp + randomness). Content-addressable IDs that change on every mutation would break graph edges and add significant complexity to the storage layer. Instead, a separate content_hash field gives drift detection and sync adapters a fast fingerprint without destabilizing the graph.

- **Hash algorithm**: Murmur3-128 — purpose-built for fingerprinting, ~10x faster than SHA-256, 32 hex chars. Not security-sensitive.
- **Hash inputs**: intent, stage, priority, complexity, and all authoring stage outputs (spark, shape, specify, decompose).
- **Hash excludes**: metadata (version, timestamps, history, created_at, updated_at).
- **ID field**: Remains a stable ULID, assigned once at creation, never changes.
- **Slug field**: Remains human-readable name, unique per graph.

## Alternatives Considered

- **Option A: Content-addressable ID (hash as the id itself)**: ID changes on every edit, requiring all graph edges to update. High complexity, breaks the graph model's stability guarantees.
- **Option C: Hash only slug + spark as identity**: Captures the "DNA" but two people sparking the same slug with different wording get different IDs. Unclear benefit over just using slug as key.
- **SHA-256 full**: Overkill — cryptographic strength not needed for change detection. 64 hex chars is noisy.
- **SHA-256 truncated**: Workable but truncation loses collision resistance unnecessarily when Murmur3-128 is purpose-built for this.

## Affects

- Proto comments on spec.proto and decision.proto (id field comments say "content-addressable")
- site/docs/concepts/specs.md (Identity section describes content-addressable IDs)
- site/docs/how-it-works.md (describes content-addressable identity)
- docs/plans/2026-02-28-client-server-architecture-design.md (Identity Scheme section)
- docs/plans/2026-02-28-vertical-slice-plan.md (proto snippet in plan)
- gen/ files will update automatically after proto changes
close_reason: Decision accepted: stable ULIDs for identity, Murmur3-128 content_hash for change detection

title: Slice 5: Spec Lifecycle
description:
Implement spec lifecycle operations (amend, supersede, abandon), drift detection, JSON Schema validation, and a spec linter. This slice adds the ability to manage specs after they've been approved and completed — reopening them for amendment, replacing them with newer specs, or abandoning them entirely. It also introduces drift detection (identifying when upstream dependencies change) and a linter for structural and graph-consistency validation.

## Scope

- **Lifecycle transitions**: amend (done→amended), supersede (any→superseded with replacement link), abandon (any→abandoned, terminal)
- **Drift detection**: dependency drift (upstream spec version changes), interface drift (placeholder), verify drift (placeholder)
- **Linter**: JSON Schema validation, dangling dependency detection, cycle detection
- **New proto service**: LifecycleService with Amend, Supersede, Abandon, CheckDrift, AcknowledgeDrift, Lint RPCs
- **Domain types**: All storage interfaces use domain types (zero proto imports in internal/storage/)
- **CLI commands**: amend, supersede, abandon, drift, drift acknowledge, lint
- **E2E test**: Full lifecycle flow through running server

## Architecture

- New LifecycleService (ConnectRPC) with dedicated storage interface
- Drift engine and linter are separate packages (internal/drift/, internal/linter/) operating on domain types
- Proto↔domain conversion in internal/server/convert.go (established pattern)
- History stored as JSON on Memgraph spec nodes
- SUPERSEDES edge type added to graph

## References

- **Revised plan**: docs/plans/2026-03-07-slice-5-spec-lifecycle-revised-plan.md
- **Original plan**: docs/plans/2026-02-28-slice-5-spec-lifecycle-plan.md (superseded — had domain types violation)
- **Roadmap design**: docs/plans/2026-02-28-vertical-slice-roadmap-design.md (Slice 5 section)
- **Implementation tracker**: docs/plans/2026-02-28-implementation-tracker.md

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] go test ./... -count=1 -timeout=120s — all pass
- [ ] golangci-lint run ./... — no issues
- [ ] buf lint — no proto issues
- [ ] go build -o specgraph ./cmd/specgraph — clean build
- [ ] CLI commands work: specgraph amend|supersede|abandon|lint|drift --help
- [ ] Full lifecycle flow: create → done → amend → supersede → abandon
