title: specgraph init should generate per-client MCP configs (skill/plugin/json) from .specgraph.yaml
description:
specgraph init should generate per-harness MCP config files in the proper location for every supported harness, from a single source of truth (.specgraph.yaml slug + server URL).

## Today (post-Tasks 34-35)

The repo carries hand-maintained MCP config files at root for each supported client:

- .cursor/mcp.json (Cursor — \${env:NAME} syntax)
- .mcp.json (Claude Code — \${NAME} syntax, "type": "http")
- opencode.json (OpenCode — {env:NAME} syntax, "type": "remote", mcp.* schema)

Drift risk: change the server URL or slug, update N files. Onboarding burden: each contributor needs to know which file applies to which client.

## Grounded survey (2026-05-04, after WebFetch verification)

| Client | Repo-committable MCP config | Plugin embeds MCP | Skill declares MCP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cursor | .cursor/mcp.json (only path) | NO — Marketplace plugins are one-click installs, not repo-distributed; Extension API is runtime-only | NO — Rules in .cursor/rules/ are instruction text only |
| Claude Code | .mcp.json at project root | technically YES (.mcp.json at plugin root) but we choose NOT to use this path for consistency with the other harnesses | NO — skills are instruction text |
| OpenCode | opencode.json at project root | NO — plugins are JS/TS modules; "no hook for MCP server configuration or management" per docs | NO — skill manifest only 5 fields |

NO client supports skill-declared MCP.

Cursor and OpenCode have ONLY one repo-committable shape (per-client root file). To avoid asymmetry where Claude Code is special-cased, we keep .mcp.json at the project root too. Consistency: every supported harness has exactly one config file at a documented project path; init generates all of them.

## Design

specgraph init (and specgraph init --update-mcp-configs as an explicit refresh) reads .specgraph.yaml (slug, server URL) and writes:

- .cursor/mcp.json (Cursor)
- .mcp.json (Claude Code)
- opencode.json (OpenCode)

Each file uses the appropriate env-var substitution syntax for that harness:
- Cursor: \${env:SPECGRAPH_API_KEY}
- Claude Code: \${SPECGRAPH_API_KEY}
- OpenCode: {env:SPECGRAPH_API_KEY}

All three include X-Specgraph-Project: <slug-from-config> and the bearer auth header.

Idempotent re-run: detect hand-edits via a header marker (e.g., `// specgraph-managed: hash=...` for JSON-with-comments where supported, or a top-level "_specgraph_managed" field where comments aren't allowed). Refuse to overwrite if hash mismatches; require --force to override.

For projects that need to deviate from the generated shape (custom server URL per developer, additional headers), document the override pattern: maintain a separate ~/.config/<harness>/mcp.json (user-level) that takes precedence over the generated project-level file in clients that merge configs.

## Codex deferred

Task 36 (Codex empirical verification) is deferred — no work-machine access to Codex for testing. When Task 36 resumes, this bead expands to add Codex's config shape to the init writer.

## Acceptance criteria

- specgraph init for a fresh project writes the three per-client configs by default.
- specgraph init --update-mcp-configs (or similar refresh flag) regenerates without touching anything else in the project.
- Single source of truth: change slug in .specgraph.yaml → re-run init → all three configs updated consistently.
- Hand-edits detected via a managed-marker; --force to overwrite.
- Docs section in CLAUDE.md and README explaining the model and how to override per-developer.
- Verification docs (docs/verification/{cursor,opencode}.md) updated to point at "specgraph init writes this" rather than "edit by hand."

## Why now / scope

This is the natural shape of Task 38 (dogfood cutover): when the SpecGraph repo itself gets specgraph init-ed, the configs we currently maintain by hand should be init-generated. So spgr-7htb is essentially the implementation of one of Task 38's deliverables. Could be tackled before, with, or as Task 38.

Phase 1 of an earlier draft (move .mcp.json into plugin/specgraph/.mcp.json) was rejected: consistency across all harnesses is more valuable than the small surface-area win of plugin-bundled MCP for Claude Code only.

## Origin

spgr-bncv Phase B Slice 5 design observation: per-client root config sprawl across Cursor + Claude Code + OpenCode (+ Codex eventually). Empirically grounded via WebFetch on cursor.com/docs/context/mcp, code.claude.com/docs/en/plugins, opencode.ai/docs/plugins/, opencode.ai/docs/skills/ on 2026-05-04.
notes: PR #929 ready for human review (https://github.com/specgraph/specgraph/pull/929). 12 commits, all CI green twice. Two rounds of CodeRabbit feedback fully resolved: (1) preflight reorder so loadGlobalCfg + ResolveServer + url-validate run before WriteProject; (2) tightened URL validation (require Host + http/https scheme), reject symlinked path components in syncOne (Critical: prevents .cursor -> ~/.config style overwrites outside projectDir), aligned design-doc step 5 wording with shipped behavior. Plus 4 internal review-driven fixes: typed Action enum, sibling-server preservation tests for Claude Code + OpenCode, partial-results-when-later-file-fails test, URL-flow-through and conflicting-arg-doesnt-mutate tests. Codex (Task 36) and dogfood cutover (Task 38) remain out of scope.

title: prime composer open_findings section calls ListFindings without slug
description:
The specgraph://prime resource composer's open_findings section calls AnalyticalPassService.ListFindings without a spec slug, but the handler requires one (returns 'invalid_argument: slug is required'). Discovered during Task 34 (Cursor MCP verification) after fixing the project-header loopback propagation bug — once project context flowed through, the open_findings section surfaced this latent issue.

## Symptom

In the rendered specgraph://prime body, the Open Findings section shows '_(unable to load: invalid_argument: slug is required)_' even when a project has specs and findings.

## Cause

ListFindings is per-spec by design. The prime composer wants project-wide open findings (across all specs in the project), and is calling ListFindings with no slug, which the handler rejects.

## Fix options

A) Add a project-scoped findings query to AnalyticalPassService — e.g., ListProjectFindings(project, status=open) — and have prime use that.
B) In the prime composer, iterate the project's specs and call ListFindings per spec, aggregating into a project-wide list. (More expensive at scale; better for now.)
C) Hybrid: aggregate-in-handler (server-side iteration) so wire traffic stays minimal.

Recommend (A) — server-side single-query is the natural shape for a project-wide question; matches how the Constitution and Graph endpoints work.

## Acceptance criteria

- specgraph://prime open_findings section returns content (or an empty list) without an error when the project has zero or more specs with findings.
- New endpoint or composer logic has tests covering: no specs, one spec with no findings, multiple specs with findings.
- No new client-side state required (composer remains stateless).

## Origin

spgr-bncv Task 34 empirical Cursor MCP verification. Fix is independent of the Task 34 PR scope; this bead is the follow-up.

title: Pin task tools golangci-lint to match CI version
description:
Currently `task tools` installs golangci-lint via `brew install golangci-lint` (Taskfile.yml:288, unpinned, gets latest), while CI pins via `go install github.com/golangci/golangci-lint/v2/cmd/golangci-lint@${{ env.GOLANGCI_LINT_VERSION }}` (.github/workflows/ci.yml:27, currently v2.12.1). Local installs drift ahead or behind CI silently, surfacing unrelated lint debt mid-slice. PR #925 hit this exactly: brew installed v2.12.1 while CI was pinned to v2.11.1, and the two versions disagreed on G115/G706/G124 enforcement. The cycle cost ~3 push iterations to resolve.

## Fix

Change `task tools` to use `go install` with the SAME pin CI uses, sourced from a single source of truth (e.g., a Taskfile var that the workflow also reads via env, or an .env file both sides import).

## Acceptance criteria

- A new local install via `task tools` produces the same golangci-lint version as CI's installed version.
- Bumping the lint version is a single-file change.
- Documented in CLAUDE.md or contributor docs how to bump.

## Why now

The v2.12.1 ruleset introduced G124 strictness on dynamic-Secure cookies and tightened nolintlint enforcement of unused directives. The next golangci-lint release will introduce more drift surface; the longer this stays unfixed, the more often slices waste cycles on phantom-debt.

title: Adopt koanf for layered config + env provider
description:
Replace the hand-rolled YAML-loader-plus-scattered-os.Getenv pattern in `internal/config/` and `cmd/specgraph/{client,serve}.go` with a single koanf-based config tree using the env provider for declarative env→config mapping.

## Why

Current state has three independent YAML loaders (`LoadGlobal`, `LoadProject`, legacy `config.Load`) and ad-hoc `os.Getenv` calls in `cmd/specgraph/client.go:90` (SPECGRAPH_API_KEY) and `cmd/specgraph/serve.go:74` (SPECGRAPH_PG_URL). Each new env var requires a hand-coded rung at the call site, and the env var name becomes permanent public interface the moment it ships.

This came up while designing Task 32 of the multi-platform plugin plan (spgr-bncv). Adding `SPECGRAPH_SERVER` as another hand-rolled rung would have made the eventual koanf migration harder by locking in a non-koanf-natural name. Decision: defer the env-var override entirely, file this bead, do koanf properly later.

## Scope

- Replace `gopkg.in/yaml.v3` direct unmarshalling with koanf + file provider.
- Add koanf env provider with a documented prefix → dot-path mapping (e.g., `SPECGRAPH_SERVER_REMOTE` → `server.remote`).
- Migrate the three existing loaders to a single koanf-loaded `*Config`.
- Remove ad-hoc `os.Getenv` calls from `client.go` and `serve.go`.
- Migrate tests off the `cfgFile` package-variable swap pattern (see `lifecycle_test.go`, `test_helpers_test.go`) onto a cleaner config-injection pattern.

## Design questions to resolve in brainstorming

- Does koanf own the config tree, or wrap the existing YAML loaders during a transitional period?
- Env naming: dot-path → underscore (`SPECGRAPH_SERVER_REMOTE`) or flatter shortcuts? Both? With aliases for the legacy `SPECGRAPH_API_KEY` / `SPECGRAPH_PG_URL` names?
- How do existing post-processing hooks (e.g., `ProbesConfig.Resolved()`) compose with koanf's struct binding?
- Migration path: one PR or several? Risk assessment.

## Out of scope

- Server URL ad-hoc override for end users — that's a downstream benefit but not the motivation. (Production users today must edit config files to override the server URL; this bead removes that friction as a side effect.)

## Probably 2-3 PRs to do well

Standalone bead. Independent of Slice 5.

title: MCP endpoint lacks auth gating — tier derived from untrusted caller input
description:
The /mcp/ HTTP handler is registered as a raw http.HandlerFunc that bypasses both auth.RequireAuth middleware and the ConnectRPC auth interceptor. Tier selection comes from X-Specgraph-MCP-Tier header or ?tier= query param, which any caller can set.

Backend RPCs enforce auth independently, so privilege escalation through MCP is bounded by what the underlying services allow. But the MCP layer itself has no identity verification — any network-reachable client can connect and select any tier.

The existing auth infrastructure (OIDC/JWT providers, composite store, role-based permissions, context-propagated Identity) is designed to handle this. The gap is wiring it into the MCP request path and deriving tier from authenticated identity rather than caller-supplied input.

Key considerations:
- The MCP client uses a loopback HTTP connection (selfBaseURL) to call ConnectRPC services — credentials need to flow through that path
- MCP protocol (mcp-go SDK) has its own session/transport lifecycle that may interact with HTTP middleware
- Tier mapping needs a policy layer connecting roles/permissions to MCP tier levels
- Stdio transport (specgraph mcp command) has a different trust model than HTTP — the caller already has local process access
close_reason: Implemented MCP auth gating: RequireAuth on /mcp/, profile derivation via OnAfterInitialize hook, context-aware credential forwarding, OIDC token store, Tier→Profile rename.

title: Site audit: comprehension gaps, stale claims, missing aha moments
description:
## Summary

Full documentation vs. code reality audit of site/docs/ completed 2026-04-10. Changes already applied for the high-confidence factual fixes (amend re-entry behavior, passes callout, conversation recording, skill count, drift scope). This issue tracks the remaining work — comprehension, story, and UX improvements.

## Already Fixed (in working tree)
- lifecycle.md: amend re-entry now correctly documents the 'lands one stage before target' behavior
- authoring.md: state machine diagram expanded to all 4 amend targets; conversation recording distinguished CLI vs skill
- passes.md: stale 'placeholder findings' callout replaced with accurate prompt-template architecture explanation; posture scheduling table added
- ecosystem.md: skill count 11 → 13
- drift.md: scope callout clarified (returns error, not silently ignored)

## Bugs (break trust immediately)
1. **Quickstart install trap**: Homebrew/binary/Docker listed as primary paths but none work yet (no published release). go install from source is the only working method but is buried in a Note callout. New visitors fail at step 1.
2. **concepts/index.md passes card wrong**: Describes linting ('detect cycles, unreachable specs') not analytical passes (red-team, peripheral vision, consistency, simplicity, constitution checks).
3. **Landing page duplicate link**: 'See the changelog for the latest release' appears twice in the same paragraph (index.md lines 89-93).

## First Glance — Value Proposition
4. **Landing page earns mechanism, not consequence**: 'queryable graph' is how it works, not why it matters. The aha moment lives in problem.md, not on the landing page.
5. **'Reference implementation' framing is distancing**: Sounds like a research artifact. Confident product language would serve better.
6. **Grid cards lead with mechanism**: 'typed edges in a queryable graph' → should lead with outcome ('query what's blocking Q3 in one command').
7. **No positive 'who is this for' statement**: Only says who NOT to use it (solo devs). Needs a positive target audience statement.

## Second Glance — Comprehension Gaps
8. **Graph queries buried**: impact/critical-path/ready/deps are the killer features and nearly invisible on the site. Need a 'Live Queries' showcase in how-it-works.
9. **how-it-works.md ends after the diagram with nothing**: No synthesis sentence, no next-steps.
10. **Posture system has no example conversation**: Drive vs Support is novel but described only in prose. Side-by-side 3-line exchanges would make it click.
11. **Constitution never shows what an agent receives**: No example of emit output or CLAUDE.md injection. The concept doesn't ground without seeing what it produces.
12. **Quickstart spec is too trivial**: healthz has no dependencies, no constraints, no drift — exercises none of the unique value propositions.
13. **No spec anatomy diagram**: No single view of all node types and edge types together. The graph model takes too long to build in the reader's head.

## Story Alignment
14. **Problem gaps don't map to concepts**: problem.md names 5 gaps (No Ground Truth, No Governance, No Addressability, No Execution Interface, No Live Query). The concepts don't close this loop explicitly.
15. **Decisions as first-class nodes is undersold**: Not on landing page cards, not in how-it-works. One of the most distinctive features in the system.
16. **SDD acronym is invoked without grounding**: Used as if reader already knows what it means.

## Diagram Improvements
17. **No before/after diagram**: file-based spec (fragile path refs) vs graph-based (typed edges, content hashes, drift) side by side.
18. **how-it-works system diagram**: Subgraph boxes try to be summary AND explanation — labels too dense. Split responsibilities: diagram shows shape, prose carries explanation.
19. **architecture.md**: 'MCP server proxy (planned)' is peer to shipped clients — creates visual noise. Should be grayed or moved to a roadmap note.

## Files Affected
- site/docs/index.md (items 3,4,5,6,7,14,15,16)
- site/docs/quickstart.md (items 1,12)
- site/docs/how-it-works.md (items 8,9,11,17,18)
- site/docs/concepts/index.md (item 2)
- site/docs/concepts/authoring.md (item 10)
- site/docs/concepts/constitution.md (item 11)
- site/docs/architecture.md (item 19)
- site/docs/concepts/specs.md or new diagram (item 13)

title: Authoring skills inconsistently record conversations - some sessions skip conversation record
description:
## Bug

When running through the authoring funnel via skills (specgraph-shape, specgraph-specify, specgraph-decompose, specgraph-approve), conversation recording is sometimes skipped entirely. The skill protocol includes a step to call `specgraph conversation record` after each stage save, but agents (particularly Sonnet) skip it when moving fast through multiple stages in a single session.

## Evidence

Three demo sessions ran through the full funnel:

1. **webhooks-funnel session (Opus)** — Conversations recorded at every stage (spark, shape, specify, decompose). Full probe/response exchanges captured.
2. **slack-amendment session (Sonnet)** — Conversations recorded for shape stage during amendment. Partial coverage.
3. **supersede session (Sonnet)** — Zero conversations recorded for event-ingestion-pipeline despite going through shape, specify, decompose, approve. `specgraph conversation list event-ingestion-pipeline` returns 'No conversation logs found.'

```
$ ./specgraph conversation list event-ingestion-pipeline
No conversation logs found.

$ ./specgraph conversation list slack-authoring-integration
### Authoring Conversation (shape, v12)
[full exchanges recorded]
```

## Root cause

The conversation recording step in the skill SKILL.md files is not enforced — it's a 'should do' instruction that agents deprioritize when context is large or they're rushing through stages. The skill doesn't gate saving on conversation recording, so the spec advances without it.

## Impact

- Conversation logs are the primary audit trail of WHY decisions were made during authoring
- Without them, the spec has the WHAT (shape output, decisions) but not the back-and-forth that led there
- The web UI Conversations section appears empty, losing the most valuable part of the authoring record

## Desired behavior

Conversation recording should be mandatory, not optional. Options:
- Server-side: require a conversation log entry for each stage transition (would break non-interactive CLI usage)
- Skill-side: make the conversation record call part of the save step itself, not a separate followup step
- Validation: add a lint rule that flags specs with stage outputs but no corresponding conversation logs

## Files involved

- plugin/specgraph/skills/specgraph-shape/SKILL.md — conversation recording instructions
- plugin/specgraph/skills/specgraph-specify/SKILL.md — same
- plugin/specgraph/skills/specgraph-decompose/SKILL.md — same
- plugin/specgraph/skills/specgraph-approve/SKILL.md — same
- Possibly plugin/specgraph/skills/specgraph-shape/references/conversation-recording.md — shared reference
close_reason: Fixed in PR #894 — conversation recording now structurally inseparable from stage persistence

title: Demo session: specgraph authoring funnel examples with asciinema recordings
description:
Tracking a demo session creating example specs through the specgraph authoring funnel, with asciinema recordings captured via cmux orchestration.

## What's been completed

### Setup proven
- cmux send/read-screen orchestration works for driving Claude Code CLI sessions
- asciinema records transparently when wrapping claude (cmux send passes through to the pty)
- Prompt detection: look for the unicode prompt character in cmux read-screen output
- Claude launched with: --dangerously-skip-permissions --plugin-dir ./plugin/specgraph --append-system-prompt (binary location hint)
- Do NOT use --bare (skips auth) or --model (let it use default)

### Spec 1: inbound-webhooks (COMPLETE - full funnel to approved)
- Recording: demo-output/webhooks-funnel.cast (6MB, covers both specs)
- Sparked with seed about GH/ADO webhook handlers for spec/slice state updates
- Shaped with 6 decisions: raw-http-not-connectrpc, provider-interface-registry, project-in-webhook-path, normalize-to-common-event, centralize-http-middleware-tech-debt, content-hash-dedup
- Specified with 26 verify criteria, 7 invariants, 17 file touches
- Decomposed with steel thread strategy: prove-pipeline + 3 parallel slices (ado-provider, dedup-and-idempotency, hardening)
- Constitution checks ran at every stage, caught real issues (error sanitization, dedup atomicity, payload size limit moved to slice 1)
- Approved after 6 checkpoints

### Spec 2: slack-authoring-integration (COMPLETE - full funnel to approved)
- Same recording file (ran in same claude session)
- Sparked as Slack-as-first-class-authoring-client (not notification pipe)
- Shaped with sidecar architecture (process boundary enforces thin-client), monorepo placement
- 5 decisions: sidecar-not-embedded, monorepo-not-separate-repo, api-key-service-account, conversation-log-for-reviews, block-kit-rendering
- Specified with 22 verify criteria, 5 invariants, 16 touches (all in cmd/specgraph-slack/)
- Decomposed with steel thread: prove-roundtrip + browse-and-search, review-and-approve, notifications
- Approved

### Amendment demo (INCOMPLETE - blocked on lifecycle design issue)
- Attempted to amend slack-authoring-integration from approved back to shape
- Discovered amend only works from done (by design, per docs)
- Other claude instance (sg-amend in workspace) made unauthorized code changes and nuked the DB by running specgraph down/init
- DB was recovered using /tmp JSON files from the original session
- Filed spgr-rth for the lifecycle nomenclature inversion

## What's remaining

### Amendment/Supersede demo recording
- Need a NEW asciinema recording (demo-output/slack-amendment.cast)
- Use supersede (current design) to replace slack-authoring-integration with expanded scope
- OR wait for spgr-rth fix and use amend (preferred, better demo)
- The new spec should expand from read/review/approve to full interactive authoring funnel for engineers
- Approach: supersede old spec, spark new one with expanded scope, shape/specify/decompose/approve

### Known environment issues
- .specgraph.yaml in repo root breaks cmd/specgraph unit tests (config discovery conflict)
- Docker/Colima testcontainers broken (ryuk reaper can't start) - affects integration tests
- The other claude instance left stale state: kill workspace 12 (sg-amend session), old specgraph binary may be stale

### cmux orchestration reference
- Create workspace: cmux new-workspace --name 'X' --cwd /Volumes/Code/github.com/specgraph-demo
- Get surface ID: cmux tree --workspace workspace:N
- Start recording+claude: cmux send --workspace W --surface S 'asciinema rec <output.cast> --overwrite -c "claude --dangerously-skip-permissions --plugin-dir ./plugin/specgraph --append-system-prompt \"The specgraph binary is at ./specgraph. Server is already running.\""'
- Send message: cmux send --workspace W --surface S 'message text here\n'
- Read screen: cmux read-screen --workspace W --surface S --scrollback --lines N
- Wait pattern: sleep N then read-screen, look for prompt char or output stability
- Exit: cmux send ... '/exit\n'
- Gotcha: if message queues ('Press up to edit queued messages'), send Escape then resend

### Specgraph server state
- Server must be running (./specgraph serve, or check ./specgraph health)
- Server runs old binary unless restarted: kill PID then ./specgraph serve &
- Do NOT run specgraph down (nukes the DB container and loses all data)
- Current DB has: slack-authoring-integration at approved, inbound-webhooks was lost in the DB nuke
- To rebuild: go build -o specgraph ./cmd/specgraph then restart server process only
notes:
2026-04-10: Final recording pass planned. All previous recordings archived in demo-output/archive/. Four recordings needed with corrected approach:

RECORDING 1: webhooks-funnel.cast — inbound-webhooks Spark→Approve
- Don't nudge the skill, let it drive naturally. Show average user interaction.
- Natural responses to probes (signal: Phase 3 coordination, scope: couple weeks GH/ADO, kill: polling sufficient or volume forces message bus)
- Push on architecture: raw HTTP not ConnectRPC, normalize to common event type, content hash dedup
- Push for steel thread decomposition over horizontal layers
- Capture conversation transcript alongside recording

RECORDING 2: slack-funnel.cast — slack-authoring Spark→Approve
- Initial spark is query/review ONLY, not full funnel (that comes in amendment)
- Guide toward Block Kit for rendering
- IMPORTANT: specgraph server supports multiple projects; slack integration should support multiple projects AND multiple servers
- Sidecar architecture, standalone process boundary
- Capture conversation transcript

RECORDING 3: slack-amendment.cast — amend slack-authoring, expand scope
- THIS is where full interactive authoring gets added
- One Slack thread per spec (conversational model)
- One Slack channel per project
- Amend from approved back to shape, re-funnel to approved
- Capture conversation transcript

RECORDING 4: supersede-demo.cast — supersede inbound-webhooks with event-ingestion-pipeline
- Claim + complete inbound-webhooks to done first
- Spark event-ingestion-pipeline as replacement
- Supersede, then shape replacement through full funnel

ENVIRONMENT SETUP:
- specgraph down, rebuild binary, specgraph serve, import constitution
- Smaller window size (user resized)
- cmux send for text, cmux send-key for enter (send + && + send-key doesn't work reliably)
- \n in cmux send sometimes creates the spec but doesn't visually submit — creates duplicate queue. Use explicit send-key enter with 2s delay.
- Escape clears queued messages but sometimes leaves text in input
- Poll with cmux read-screen every 10s max
- Each recording: asciinema rec demo-output/<name>.cast --overwrite -c 'claude --dangerously-skip-permissions --name <session> --plugin-dir ./plugin/specgraph --append-system-prompt "..."'
- Append system prompt must include: binary at ./specgraph, server running, do NOT restart/down/make code changes

KNOWN ISSUES:
- cmux send with \n sometimes silently creates specs without visual submission — creates already_exists on retry. Check specgraph list if confused.
- slack-authoring-integration slug may be taken (abandoned from previous runs). Use slack-authoring or let the skill pick a slug.
- Constitution check background agents can block the flow — tell instance to skip if waiting too long.
- The skill sometimes uses interactive selectors, sometimes free-text prompts. Need to handle both.

title: Interface and verify drift detection
description: Extend the drift detection engine beyond dependency drift. Currently only DriftTypeDependency is implemented (comparing content_hash_at_link on DEPENDS_ON edges vs current upstream hash). The DriftTypeInterface and DriftTypeVerify types are defined in internal/storage/lifecycle.go but not implemented. Needs: (1) Define what interface drift means — likely detecting breaking changes in a spec's public contract (API surface, data schemas) that downstream consumers haven't acknowledged. (2) Define what verify drift means — likely automated checks that a spec's claims still hold (test results, deployment state). (3) Implement detection algorithms for both in internal/drift/drift.go. (4) Add CLI support (specgraph drift --scope interfaces|verify). (5) Storage for interface/verify baselines. Related but distinct from spgr-93k (code-level drift which watches repo file changes).

title: Multi-layer constitution composition and provenance
description: Finish layered constitution support. Currently the schema stores a layer field and the proto defines User/Org/Project/Domain layers, but only one constitution per project is supported (enforced by unique constraint on project_slug in migration 003). Needs: (1) Remove or relax the unique constraint to allow multiple constitutions per project (one per layer). (2) Layer-specific CRUD in ConstitutionBackend interface (Get/Update by layer, ListLayers). (3) Merge algorithm that composes layers with more-specific-overrides-general semantics. (4) Provenance trail so every resolved value carries which layer set it. (5) CLI support for layer-aware import/show/emit. (6) Update PrimeData to use merged constitution. See site/docs/concepts/constitution.md for the documented design intent.

title: Add steel thread decomposition pattern
description: Add a new decomp style/pattern: steel thread — a thin vertical slice through the problem that proves out interfaces and core concepts end-to-end, maximizing parallelism for subsequent work. This pattern focuses on cutting through all layers (proto, storage, server, CLI) with minimal scope to validate integration points before broadening.

title: Auto-run analytical passes via Claude Code hooks after stage transitions
description:
The passRegistry in internal/authoring/passes.go defines which analytical passes should auto-run at each funnel stage+posture (constitution_check at every stage, peripheral_vision at shape, red_team+consistency at specify, simplicity at decompose). Currently the server only serves prompts via RunAnalyticalPass RPC — nothing triggers them automatically.

Design and implement a client-side mechanism in the specgraph Claude Code plugin that:
1. Detects when an authoring stage transition completes (e.g., after StoreSparkOutput, StoreShapeOutput, etc.)
2. Queries PassesForStage to get the auto-run passes for the current stage+posture
3. Dispatches each pass as a subagent (parallel where independent)
4. Each subagent calls RunAnalyticalPass to get the prompt, executes it, calls StoreFindings with results
5. Reports findings back to the authoring conversation

Implementation options to evaluate:
- Claude Code PostToolUse hook on the authoring RPCs (Spark, Shape, Specify, Decompose) that triggers pass dispatch
- Skill-level orchestration in the specgraph-spark/shape/specify/decompose skills that calls passes after storing output
- A dedicated 'analytical-pass-runner' agent in the plugin that watches for stage changes

The ChangeSubscriber/event system in the storage layer could also notify, but that's server-side and doesn't have access to Claude Code subagents. Client-side hooks are the right seam.

title: CreateDecision returns 500 instead of 409 on duplicate slug (Postgres)
description: CreateDecision in internal/storage/postgres/decision.go does not handle PG unique constraint violation (SQLSTATE 23505) for the (project_slug, slug) PK. When a decision with the same slug already exists, the INSERT fails with a raw PG error that bubbles up as 500 Internal Error instead of mapping to ErrDecisionAlreadyExists (409 AlreadyExists). PR #824 added the sentinel error and handler mapping but the storage layer catch was only added for the scan path. Need to also verify CreateSpec has the same issue. Found during UI Playwright e2e tests where seedDecision retries expected 409 but got 500.

title: GetFullGraph logs duplicate slugs to stderr instead of using slog
description:
In graph.go:440-442:

  fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "WARNING: duplicate node slug %q in project %q — data integrity issue\n", slug, s.project)

The rest of the codebase uses log/slog (e.g. slog.Error in tx.go:53, slog.Error in migrate.go:29). Direct writes to os.Stderr bypass the structured logger, skip log level filtering, and are untestable. Also requires an 'os' import that would otherwise not be needed.

File: internal/storage/postgres/graph.go, lines 9 and 440-442.

Fix: replace with slog.Warn("duplicate node slug in project", "slug", slug, "project", s.project) and remove the 'os' import.

title: migrate.go declares duplicate package doc comment already in postgres.go
description:
Both postgres.go (line 4) and migrate.go (line 4) declare:

  // Package postgres implements storage backends using PostgreSQL.
  package postgres

Having the package doc comment on migrate.go in addition to postgres.go is redundant and may cause 'revive' to emit a warning. Per CLAUDE.md: 'revive requires package comments — new Go packages need a package doc comment on the first .go file'. The convention is to have it on exactly one file (typically the main entry-point file).

File: internal/storage/postgres/migrate.go, line 4.

Fix: remove the package doc comment from migrate.go, keeping it only in postgres.go.

title: marshalTagsForDelta and marshalRejectedAltsForDelta silently return '[]' on marshal error
description: decision.go:78-103 — marshalTagsForDelta and marshalRejectedAltsForDelta both silently return '[]' on json.Marshal error. These are used for field-delta computation in changelog entries. While json.Marshal on []string or []struct rarely fails in practice, silent fallback to '[]' means a changelog entry would incorrectly record an empty value instead of the actual tags/alternatives, making audit history misleading. Fix: since these are internal helpers called from contexts that can return errors, propagate the error or at minimum log it at slog.Warn level.

title: UpdateSyncState stores external error messages without size bound
description:
File: internal/storage/postgres/sync.go, UpdateSyncState (~line 66–81).

The errorMessage parameter is sourced from external sync adapter results (e.g., GitHub API error bodies) and is stored directly in the error_message column of sync_mappings without any length constraint:

    tag, err := s.exec(ctx,
        `UPDATE sync_mappings
         SET state = $1, error_message = $2, last_sync = $3
         WHERE spec_slug = $4 AND project_slug = $5 AND adapter = $6`,
        string(state), errorMessage, now, ...
    )

The schema definition (migration 001) does not impose a maximum length on error_message (TEXT column with no CHECK constraint). A malicious or misconfigured sync adapter could supply a multi-megabyte error string that bloats the database.

Additionally, error responses from external adapters may contain internal stack traces or sensitive path information that should be sanitized before storage.

Suggested fix:
- Truncate errorMessage to a reasonable maximum (e.g., 4096 bytes) before storage: errorMessage = truncateString(errorMessage, 4096).
- Optionally add a CHECK constraint (error_message_len CHECK (octet_length(error_message) <= 4096)) in a migration.

title: Connection string passed raw to two separate driver constructors with no credential masking in errors
description:
File: internal/storage/postgres/postgres.go (New, ~line 74–84) and migrate.go (runMigrations, ~line 21).

The raw connString (which typically contains a username:password@ segment) is passed to both pgxpool.New and sql.Open in sequence. Neither call wraps errors with the connString in the message, so credentials are not directly leaked in error returns.

However, there is no scrubbing/masking of the connection string before it passes to these libraries. If either library logs the DSN internally at debug level (pgx v5 does not by default, but this is library-version-dependent), or if a panic/trace includes the call arguments, the password embedded in the DSN could appear in logs.

Additionally, the raw connString is stored for the lifetime of the New() call stack — it is never zeroed after use.

Suggested improvement:
1. Parse the connection string into a pgxpool.Config (pgxpool.ParseConfig) and pass the config struct to pgxpool.NewWithConfig. This avoids ever holding the raw DSN string beyond initial parse.
2. For the migration path, use pgxpool.ParseConfig to extract the DSN and pass it to sql.Open, then zero the raw string.
3. Provide a helper that masks the password segment for use in error messages if needed.

title: GetFullGraph leaks internal project slug and node slug to stderr on data integrity violation
description:
File: internal/storage/postgres/graph.go, GetFullGraph (~line 440).

    fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "WARNING: duplicate node slug %q in project %q — data integrity issue\n", slug, s.project)

In a server process, os.Stderr is often captured by a log aggregator and stored in a log management system (e.g., CloudWatch, Loki). This line emits both the project slug and a node slug — internal identifiers — into that stream without structured logging, bypassing any log redaction pipeline.

In the Memgraph counterpart, slog.Warn is used for similar diagnostics, which goes through the structured logging stack where field-level redaction can be applied.

Suggested fix: replace with a structured slog.Warn call:

    slog.Warn("duplicate node slug detected",
        "slug", slug,
        "project", s.project,
    )

This is consistent with the rest of the package (tx.go uses slog.Error for rollback/panic events) and allows the caller's slog handler to apply any necessary field sanitization or sampling.

title: GetFullGraph duplicate-slug warning goes to os.Stderr, not slog
description: graph.go:440-442 — When a duplicate node slug is detected (data integrity violation), the code calls fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, ...) directly. The rest of the codebase uses slog throughout. Using os.Stderr bypasses any log aggregation, level filtering, or structured logging the operator has configured, making this warning invisible in production environments that capture only slog output. Fix: replace with slog.Warn(...) using structured key-value fields (slug, project).

title: storeJSONColumn interpolates column name into SQL without parameterization
description:
File: internal/storage/postgres/authoring.go, function storeJSONColumn (~line 416).

The column name is interpolated directly into the SQL string via fmt.Sprintf:

    sql := fmt.Sprintf(
        "UPDATE specs SET %s = $1, updated_at = $2 WHERE slug = $3 AND project_slug = $4",
        column,
    )

The values are fully parameterized ($1–$4), so this is not a classic SQL injection. The column name is guarded by two defenses: (1) an allowlist check against allowedJSONColumns map, and (2) a character-class validation loop that rejects anything outside [a-zA-Z0-9_].

However, the column name is still placed directly in the SQL string rather than being selected from a switch-case that emits a hardcoded literal. This is a defense-in-depth gap: if either guard were bypassed or the allowlist were to be widened with an unsafe entry, arbitrary column names could flow into SQL.

Suggested fix: replace the fmt.Sprintf pattern with an explicit switch that maps known column names to hardcoded SQL string literals, eliminating the string interpolation entirely:

    switch column {
    case "spark_output":
        sql = "UPDATE specs SET spark_output = $1, ..."
    case "shape_output":
        ...
    default:
        return fmt.Errorf("disallowed column: %q", column)
    }

title: docs/superpowers/specs design doc describes 'sync beads <slug>' with a slug arg that was never implemented
description:
docs/superpowers/specs/2026-03-27-site-docs-feature-coverage-design.md Workstream 3 (guides/sync.md) line ~1415 says:

  '1. Beads sync — sync beads <slug>, dry-run mode...'
  '2. GitHub sync — sync github <slug>...'

The actual implementation takes no slug (sync operates on all matching specs). The guides/sync.md correctly documents this ('there is no slug argument'). The design doc itself is stale relative to the implementation.

This is an internal planning document, so the impact is low, but future readers of the design doc may be confused about the intended interface.

Suggested fix: Update the spec doc lines to remove the '<slug>' argument from sync beads/github descriptions.

title: docs.go writeGroups silently skips docs/help/completion from Other section with no comment explaining why
Unchecked fmt.Fprintf return value inconsistent with project errcheck pattern
description:
cmd/specgraph/docs.go line 73: fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, ...) return values are silently discarded without blank-assignment. The project's errcheck linter config has check-blank: true. The established pattern in this package (see init.go:30) is to use _, _ = fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, ...) when intentionally ignoring the return value. The new code is inconsistent with that pattern and may trigger errcheck in CI.

Fix: Change line 73 to:
  _, _ = fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "CLI reference written to %s\n", outPath)

File: cmd/specgraph/docs.go line 73

title: Review: PR #697 — docs: site docs feature coverage

title: startFakeConstitutionServer duplicates startFakeServer logic instead of delegating to it
description:
File: cmd/specgraph/constitution_test.go, lines 607-620 of diff (new code added to an existing file).

startFakeConstitutionServer manually inlines the full server-startup-and-config-file logic — the same boilerplate that was introduced in test_helpers_test.go as the generic startFakeServer[H any] helper (diff lines 3126-3140) precisely to avoid this duplication. All other per-service helpers (startFakeSpecServer, startFakeGraphServer, etc.) delegate to startFakeServer; constitution_test.go does not.

Suggested fix — replace the inline implementation with a one-liner like the other wrappers:

  func startFakeConstitutionServer(t *testing.T, h specgraphv1connect.ConstitutionServiceHandler) {
      t.Helper()
      startFakeServer[specgraphv1connect.ConstitutionServiceHandler](t, h, specgraphv1connect.NewConstitutionServiceHandler)
  }

The inconsistency also means any future change to the server-startup logic (e.g., adding TLS, auth headers, or timeout config) must be applied in two places instead of one.

2. runAmend / runSupersede / runAbandon — have happy-path tests in lifecycle_test.go but no RPC error tests. Errors are wrapped as 'amend:', 'supersede:', 'abandon:' respectively.

3. runLint — has happy-path and client-error but no RPC error test. Error is wrapped as 'lint:'.

These are all low-risk omissions since the functions are simple wrappers, but completing the pattern would make the test suite consistent and catch future wrapping regressions.

title: Inconsistent use of flag-restore helpers across test files
description:
conversation_test.go correctly introduces setConvRecordVars and setConvListVars helpers that save/restore package-level flag variables and register cleanup. However, most other test files (findings_test.go, graph_test.go, decision_test.go, edge_test.go, status_test.go, progress_test.go) repeat the same old/new/Cleanup pattern inline for every test, sometimes for pairs of variables.

Examples of the repetitive inline pattern (findings_test.go):
    oldPT := findingsListPassType
    oldJSON := findingsListJSON
    findingsListPassType = ""
    findingsListJSON = false
    t.Cleanup(func() {
        findingsListPassType = oldPT
        findingsListJSON = oldJSON
    })

This block appears 6 times in findings_test.go alone with only the assigned values changing.

A setFindingsListVars(t, passType string, jsonOut bool) helper (mirroring setConvListVars) would eliminate ~30 lines of boilerplate in that file. Similarly for graph commands with paired bool flags.

This is a consistency issue, not just a brevity issue — new contributors will follow whichever pattern they see first, leading to further divergence.

Files with the issue:
- cmd/specgraph/findings_test.go (6 repetitions)
- cmd/specgraph/graph_test.go (multiple paired flag restores)
- cmd/specgraph/decision_test.go (decisionListStatus/decisionListJSON)
- cmd/specgraph/edge_test.go (edgeListType/edgeListJSON)
close_reason: PR #672 merged. Severity:suggestion — inline flag-restore pattern still repeated across test files instead of using helpers. Review cycle complete.

title: Ignored os.Chdir error in test cleanup may corrupt subsequent tests' working directory
description:
In cmd/specgraph/constitution_test.go, TestRunConstitutionEmit_HappyPath_File uses os.Chdir to change the process working directory and registers a cleanup that silently discards the error:

    t.Cleanup(func() { _ = os.Chdir(origDir) })

If this cleanup call fails (e.g., origDir was deleted, or a prior test left the process in an uncleanable state), all subsequent tests in the same package run will inherit the wrong working directory. Tests that write config files using relative paths or that call filepath.Join with relative components would then write to an unexpected location. In a test environment this is unlikely to be exploitable, but it represents a fragile isolation boundary.

Additionally, os.Chdir is a process-wide syscall. Even though no test in this PR calls t.Parallel(), any future addition of t.Parallel() to this or neighboring tests would cause a race on the global working directory.

Suggested fix:
1. Check and log or fail on the cleanup error:
   t.Cleanup(func() { require.NoError(t, os.Chdir(origDir)) })
   or at minimum:
   t.Cleanup(func() {
       if err := os.Chdir(origDir); err != nil {
           t.Errorf("cleanup: chdir back to %s: %v", origDir, err)
       }
   })
2. Consider restructuring the test to avoid os.Chdir entirely by passing an absolute output path to runConstitutionEmit, or by stubbing the cwd lookup used in the production function.

title: Test uses ambiguous 'p1'/'p2' strings as priority values
description:
In TestCreateSpec_DuplicateSlugReturnsError (internal/storage/memgraph/memgraph_test.go:162-165), the priority and complexity arguments use the strings "p1", "medium" for the first call and "p2", "low" for the second.

The string "p1" looks like a project-ID shorthand (matching the pattern used in ListSpecs tests below, where "p1"/"p2" are passed as the priority filter). Using "p1" as a priority value is misleading to readers who see newStore always binding project="test". The second call using "p2" as priority compounds this.

Suggested: use unambiguous priority literals like "medium"/"high" for both calls. This makes it immediately clear the two CreateSpec calls differ only in intent/priority/complexity, not in project, which is the crux of the test — same slug, same project, different content should still be rejected.

title: existsParams is redundant — reuse params for existence check
description:
In CreateSpec (internal/storage/memgraph/memgraph.go:196), a separate existsParams map is built by calling mergeParams(s.projectParam(), map[string]any{"slug": slug}). However, params (built at line 174) already contains both $project and $slug alongside all other CREATE parameters. The existence check query only uses $project and $slug, so params can be passed directly to executeQuery instead of constructing a redundant intermediate map.

Current (lines 196-197):
  existsParams := mergeParams(s.projectParam(), map[string]any{"slug": slug})
  existing, existsErr := s.executeQuery(txCtx, existsQuery, existsParams)

Suggested:
  existing, existsErr := s.executeQuery(txCtx, existsQuery, params)

This reduces one mergeParams call and one map allocation per CreateSpec invocation, and eliminates the separate variable.
close_reason: Fixed: slug_key constraint + error sanitization + existsParams reuse

title: Review: PR #615 — fix: enforce slug uniqueness in CreateSpec
description: Review of PR #615: Adds pre-check query in CreateSpec transaction to return ErrSpecAlreadyExists for duplicate slugs. Fixes e2e tests that relied on silently creating duplicates.

title: Spark skill: check for existing/similar slugs before creating spec
description: The spark skill should run specgraph list and check for exact and substring matches against the proposed slug before calling specgraph spark. This prevents duplicate specs (exact match) and catches near-misses like webhook-notifications vs webhook-stage-notifications (substring/prefix match). Approach: skill greps the list output, presents matches to user with option to resume existing spec or proceed with new one. No code changes needed -- skill-only fix layering on top of spgr-ecm (code-level uniqueness enforcement). Fuzzy/semantic matching deferred to spgr-1nk (vector search).

title: SpecifyOutput proto loses structured detail — interface contract, verify criteria flatten to strings
description:
The SpecifyOutput proto message uses simple strings (interfaceContract as string, verifyCriteria/invariants/touches as repeated string). During the specify stage, the skill produces rich structured content: proto service definitions, Go interfaces, HTTP delivery formats, test criteria with categories (emission, CRUD, delivery, filtering, retry, e2e), file tables with purpose/tier columns. All of this flattens into plain strings when persisted.

Direction: We need to revisit the structure to allow more structured detail that is free-form but defined. Not rigid typed fields for every possible content shape, but structured containers that preserve the richness of what the skill produces. Think: named sections with typed content (e.g., InterfaceSection with name + body, VerifyCriterion with category + description + tier, FileTouch with path + purpose + change_type).

The goal is: free-form enough that skills can put whatever they need, but defined enough that downstream consumers (execution bundles, analytical passes, graph queries) can navigate the content programmatically.

Discovered during demo walkthrough where the specify stage produced excellent structured output that couldn't survive the proto roundtrip.

title: SpecifyOutput.interface_contract is too narrow for multi-surface APIs
description: The interface_contract field in SpecifyOutput is a single string. For specs with multiple API surfaces (proto service, Go interface, HTTP delivery format, circuit breaker state machine), all detail gets flattened into one string during specify stage upload. This loses the structured presentation from the conversation. Consider: (1) making interface_contract a repeated string or structured message, (2) adding a separate 'api_surfaces' repeated message with name/contract pairs, (3) or keeping the string but documenting that markdown formatting is preserved. Discovered during demo: the webhook-notifications spec has 3 distinct surfaces (WebhookService proto, EventBus Go interface, outbound HTTP delivery) that don't fit cleanly in one string.

This lets projects customize how analytical passes evaluate their specs without forking the binary. Implementation approach: at template load time in RunAnalyticalPass, check for a local override file first and fall back to the embedded default.

title: findingSeverityToProto silent fallback to UNSPECIFIED is undocumented
description:
File: internal/server/analytical_pass_handler.go, findingSeverityToProto function (around line 916).

The function comment reads:
  // findingSeverityToProto converts a domain FindingSeverity to the proto type.

The companion function findingSeverityFromProto explicitly documents: 'Returns an error for UNSPECIFIED or unknown numeric values.' However, findingSeverityToProto silently returns FINDING_SEVERITY_UNSPECIFIED for unknown/invalid domain severity values rather than panicking or logging. This asymmetry means a stored finding with a bad severity string would be silently converted to UNSPECIFIED when listed, masking a data integrity issue.

The comment should document this behavior: add 'Unknown values map to FINDING_SEVERITY_UNSPECIFIED.' to make the asymmetry with findingSeverityFromProto explicit and intentional to future readers.

title: FindingSeverity typed string allows invalid zero value to escape storage layer
description:
internal/storage/authoring.go: FindingSeverity is a typed string with three named constants (SeverityCritical, SeverityWarning, SeverityNote). The zero value FindingSeverity("") is constructible without error.

In memgraph/findings.go ListFindings, the severity is populated via:
    storage.FindingSeverity(severity)
where severity is a raw string from the database. If a Finding node was stored with an empty severity (e.g., via the PassType bug described in the critical finding), this surfaces as FindingSeverity("") in the returned struct with no error.

There is no validation in ListFindings that the deserialized FindingSeverity is a known value. A consumer iterating findings has no way to know if FindingSeverity("") is intentional.

This is a pre-existing pattern in the codebase (other typed strings have the same property), but because AnalyticalFinding is new and the handler's findingSeverityFromProto correctly rejects UNSPECIFIED, the round-trip guarantee is only as strong as the storage layer's write path. If an invalid severity escapes into the DB, reads will silently return corrupt data.

Suggested: add a ValidFindingSeverity check in ListFindings (parallel to ValidPassType), or validate in the handler before returning to clients.
close_reason: Already fixed — findingSeverityFromProto and findingSeverityToProto both return errors for zero/unknown values

title: Proto pass_type as string bypasses type system; misses enum enforcement at wire layer
description:
proto/specgraph/v1/analytical_pass.proto: pass_type is typed as string in AnalyticalFinding (field 2), StoreFindingsRequest (field 2), and ListFindingsRequest (field 2), while severity uses a proper proto enum (FindingSeverity).

This asymmetry means:
- An API client sending an unknown pass_type gets a runtime CodeInvalidArgument error deep in the handler rather than a parseable enum.
- Generated client code provides no discoverability of valid pass types.
- The server-side ValidPassType() function has to re-implement what a proto enum would give for free.

The design doc acknowledges this is pre-1.0, and adding passes is deliberately decoupled from proto changes. However, a PassType enum in the proto would cost little and remove an entire validation class. The existing pattern in the codebase (Posture, FindingSeverity, ConstitutionLayer) consistently uses proto enums for closed sets.

Suggested: add enum PassType { PASS_TYPE_UNSPECIFIED = 0; PASS_TYPE_CONSTITUTION_CHECK = 1; ... } to analytical_pass.proto. Use it in StoreFindingsRequest.pass_type, ListFindingsRequest.pass_type, RunAnalyticalPassRequest.pass_name, and AnalyticalFinding.pass_type. Remove ValidPassType() from the storage layer — the enum's zero value (UNSPECIFIED) handles the validation.
close_reason: Fixed — handler tests added, proto enum, CI hardening, length limits

title: StoreFindings handler does not set PassType on domain findings — field sent to storage is always zero-valued
description:
internal/server/analytical_pass_handler.go StoreFindings (lines 103-118) builds the domain slice but never populates the PassType field:

  domain[i] = storage.AnalyticalFinding{
      Severity:   sev,
      Summary:    f.Summary,
      Detail:     f.Detail,
      Constraint: f.Constraint,
      Resolution: f.Resolution,
      // PassType is zero-valued / missing
  }

The storage implementation (memgraph/findings.go) ignores AnalyticalFinding.PassType and reads pass type from the function parameter instead, so this does not cause a bug today. However, the domain struct carries a PassType field precisely to encode which pass a finding belongs to. Leaving it zero-valued means any code that inspects the domain slice before or after storage (e.g., logging, future in-memory backends) will see an empty PassType.

Fix: add PassType: storage.PassType(msg.PassType) to the domain[i] assignment, matching how the integration test backend (analytical_pass_handler_test.go line 1021) populates this field.
close_reason: Duplicate of spgr-bp7.6 (same PassType zero value finding)

title: StoreFindings accepts unbounded free-text fields (summary, detail, constraint, resolution) with no length limits
description: internal/server/analytical_pass_handler.go, StoreFindings method, and proto/specgraph/v1/analytical_pass.proto StoreFindingsRequest: the Summary, Detail, Constraint, and Resolution fields on AnalyticalFindingInput have no documented or enforced maximum length. LLM outputs fed through this path can be arbitrarily large. There is also no cap on the number of findings per request. Both paths flow into Memgraph node properties. Fix: impose reasonable upper bounds on field lengths (e.g. 1 KB for summary, 64 KB for detail) and a maximum finding count per request (e.g. 100), returning CodeInvalidArgument when exceeded. This prevents storage exhaustion from runaway LLM output or abusive callers.
close_reason: Fixed — handler tests added, proto enum, CI hardening, length limits

title: memgraph StoreFindings: no test for ListFindings spec-not-found at storage layer

The integration tests in findings_test.go test StoreFindings on a nonexistent spec (TestStoreFindings_SpecNotFound) but have no equivalent test for ListFindings. This means the handler's CodeNotFound branch for ListFindings (analytical_pass_handler.go line ~864) is dead code that cannot currently be exercised. Either ListFindings should verify spec existence and return ErrSpecNotFound (making the handler branch live), or the discrepancy should be documented. Add TestListFindings_SpecNotFound to findings_test.go.
close_reason: Addressed by fixing spgr-bp7.9 — adding GetSpec guard to ListFindings also adds the test

title: nscloud-cache-action uses floating @v1 tag instead of pinned SHA
description:
In .github/workflows/ci.yml (and release.yml), the new Namespace cache action is referenced as namespacelabs/nscloud-cache-action@v1 — a floating major-version tag. The project's other actions use either pinned SHAs (actions/checkout@v6 style is consistent in the repo, though v6 is itself a floating tag) or a specific version. The project CLAUDE.md instructs to verify GitHub Actions versions before use.

Using a floating tag means the action can change behavior on re-run without a diff. For a cache action this is lower risk than for a build/deploy action, but it is inconsistent with the project's stated verification requirement.

Fix: Pin to a specific commit SHA from the namespacelabs/nscloud-cache-action repository, e.g.:
  uses: namespacelabs/nscloud-cache-action@<sha>
close_reason: Already fixed — all nscloud actions pinned to full commit SHAs (same as bp7.4)

title: rumdl installation now downloads a binary from GitHub via curl-pipe-tar with no checksum verification
description: ci.yml, 'Install rumdl' step: the PR replaces 'pip install rumdl==' with a curl-pipe-tar pattern that downloads a binary from github.com releases and extracts it directly to /usr/local/bin. There is no cryptographic checksum or signature verification step. While the URL includes the version string (pinned via env.RUMDL_VERSION), an attacker who can intercept the download or who compromises the GitHub release asset can substitute a malicious binary. Fix: after downloading, verify a known SHA256 checksum for the specific release asset before extraction, or use a sigstore-signed artifact if the project provides one. This is a defense-in-depth concern; the immediate risk is lower because RUMDL_VERSION is a pinned env var, but the no-verify curl-pipe pattern is a security anti-pattern for CI.
close_reason: Fixed — handler tests added, proto enum, CI hardening, length limits

title: quickstart.md: specgraph init implicitly starts the server, splitting them into two steps is misleading
description:
site/docs/quickstart.md presents 'Start the Server' (specgraph up) and 'Initialize a Project' (specgraph init) as two independent steps. However, cmd/specgraph/init.go (runInit function, line 29) calls runUp() as its first action, meaning specgraph init already starts the server. Running specgraph up first and then specgraph init means the server start is attempted twice; the second attempt silently short-circuits because isServerHealthy() returns true.

This ordering isn't broken (it works), but the explanation is misleading — a user reading the guide will think 'Start the Server' and 'Initialize a Project' are independent concerns. In practice, init does both.

Suggestion: Explain that specgraph init handles both initialization and server startup, and present specgraph up as the command for restarting the server after a reboot rather than as a prerequisite separate step.

title: .beads/push-state.json is a machine-local runtime file that should not be committed
description:
File: .beads/push-state.json (new file, diff lines 9-19)

This file records local sync state (last_push timestamp, last_commit hash). It is machine-specific and has no value when checked into a shared repository. The .beads/.gitignore already excludes sync-state.json and export-state/ but not push-state.json.

This is analogous to .envrc (which the same PR correctly adds to .gitignore).

Fix: Remove .beads/push-state.json from the PR and add 'push-state.json' to .beads/.gitignore.

title: .beads/push-state.json committed — machine-local sync state exposes internal remote ref layout
description:
File: .beads/push-state.json

Contents:
  {
    "remote": "specgraph/specgraph",
    "ref": "refs/dolt/data",
    "last_push_hash": ""
  }

The .beads/.gitignore lists sync-state.json as ignored (as 'local-only, per-machine') but does NOT list push-state.json. This appears to be an oversight — push-state.json is analogous sync state tracking local push progress.

Concerns:
- This is machine-local state that will cause conflicts or stale state for other contributors who clone and run bd.
- It exposes the internal Dolt ref path (refs/dolt/data) used for the beads sync backend, which is minor information disclosure.
- The .beads/.gitignore comment explicitly calls out that these files are 'machine-specific and should not be shared across clones' — push-state.json fits that description.

Remediation:
Add push-state.json to .beads/.gitignore alongside sync-state.json.

title: ChangeLog graph nodes with field deltas for version tracking
description:
## Decision

Track every material change to specs via field-level deltas on HistoryEntry, with stage transitions marked as checkpoints. Defer compaction and snapshot infrastructure.

## Rationale

- Field deltas capture lossless change information at write time with minimal overhead
- Content hash is already computed on every mutation, making delta detection nearly free
- Deltas are NOT reconstructible after the fact — capturing them now preserves information we cannot recover later
- Avoids new graph node types (SpecVersion) in favor of extending existing HistoryEntry
- Snapshots are reconstructible from deltas, but not vice versa
- Compaction is optional infrastructure that can be added when history arrays grow large enough to matter

## Alternatives Considered

- **Option A (last_synced_hash on SyncMapping)**: Too narrow — only covers sync adapters, not core versioning. Still needed for sync, but not sufficient alone.
- **Option B (edge versioning with established_at_version property)**: Deferred — edge staleness derivable from spec hash changes without storing version on edges.
- **Option C (SpecVersion vertex nodes)**: Deferred to Phase 3/4 — heaviest approach, full git-for-specs in the graph. Only needed if full content snapshots at every version become a real requirement.
- **Checkpoint snapshots only (no field deltas)**: Rejected — loses the "what exactly changed" information between checkpoints. Hashes tell you IF something changed; deltas tell you WHAT.
- **Full snapshots on every entry**: Rejected — storage bloat for data reconstructible from deltas.

## Design

### Proto Changes
- New message: FieldChange { field, old_value, new_value }
- New fields on HistoryEntry: content_hash (string), checkpoint (bool), repeated FieldChange changes

### Behavior
1. On every mutation where ContentHash() changes → append HistoryEntry with field deltas
2. On stage transition → set checkpoint=true on the new entry
3. Compaction (DEFERRED) → collapse non-checkpoint entries, merging changesets

### Build Now
- FieldChange proto message
- content_hash, checkpoint, changes fields on HistoryEntry
- Delta computation in storage layer

### Defer
- Compaction logic and compacted flag
- Merge semantics for changesets
- Snapshot fields on HistoryEntry

## Affects

- spgr-1p6 (version tracking strategy)
- spgr-3ei (drift detection refactor to version comparison)
design:
## Revised Design: ChangeLog Graph Nodes

### Strategy (revised from original)
Changed from Approach 1 (field deltas on HistoryEntry JSON blob) to Approach 2 (ChangeLog as first-class graph nodes). 

### Rationale for pivot
- Cross-spec queries ("what changed this week?", "which specs drifted since approval?") require full-graph scans + JSON deserialization with blob approach
- ChangeLog nodes are natively queryable in Cypher — single-hop, indexable, no application-level filtering
- Write cost is negligible — single atomic Cypher query creates ChangeLog node alongside Spec update
- Eliminates maxHistoryEntries cap concern entirely
- Aligns with graph-native philosophy: if it's queryable, it's a node

### Proto Changes
- New message: FieldChange { field, old_value, new_value }
- New message: ChangeLog { version, stage, content_hash, checkpoint, date, summary, reason, repeated FieldChange changes }
- New edge type: HAS_CHANGE (Spec → ChangeLog)

### Existing history_json
- Stays for now as backward-compatible lifecycle audit log
- Migration to ChangeLog nodes is a future consideration, not a blocker
close_reason: Implemented: PR #41 merged — ChangeLog graph nodes with field deltas

title: Design: Version tracking strategy for content hash change detection
description:
The content_hash field (spgr-hya) provides a fingerprint but needs a comparison point to detect change/drift. Three options to evaluate:

**Option A: last_synced_hash on SyncMapping**
- Sync adapters store hash at last sync time
- Lightweight, scoped to sync use case only

**Option B: Edge versioning (established_at_version property)**
- Every edge records the spec version when it was created
- Enables "what changed since this edge was established?"
- Moderate overhead (property on every edge write)

**Option C: SpecVersion vertex (content_hash as ID)**
- Each mutation creates a (:SpecVersion) node linked to (:Spec) via [:HAS_VERSION]
- Full history as a chain of version nodes — git-for-specs in the graph
- Heaviest approach but most queryable

Note: Spec already has `version` (int32) and `history` (HistoryEntry array). Any strategy should complement or replace those, not duplicate them.

Depends on content_hash being implemented first (spgr-lmt).
design:
## Design: Two-tier History with Field Deltas

### Strategy
Track every material change as a HistoryEntry with field-level deltas. Mark stage transitions as checkpoints. Defer compaction.

### Proto Changes
- New message: FieldChange { field, old_value, new_value }
- New fields on HistoryEntry: content_hash (string), checkpoint (bool), repeated FieldChange changes

### How it works
1. On every mutation where ContentHash() changes: append HistoryEntry with field deltas
2. On stage transition: set checkpoint=true on the new entry
3. Compaction (DEFERRED): collapse non-checkpoint entries between stages, merging changesets

### What we build now
- FieldChange proto message
- content_hash, checkpoint, changes fields on HistoryEntry
- Delta computation in storage layer (compare old vs new fields at write time)

### What we defer
- Compaction logic and compacted flag
- Merge semantics for changesets
- Snapshot fields on HistoryEntry
- Diff-between-stages query/UI

### Rationale
- Field deltas are data capture, not infrastructure — cheap and lossless at write time
- Content hash is already computed on every mutation, so delta detection is nearly free
- Compaction is optional and can be added when history arrays grow large enough to matter
- Snapshots are reconstructible from deltas; deltas are NOT reconstructible from snapshots
close_reason: Implemented: ChangeLog graph nodes with field deltas

title: OIDC authentication: Azure Entra ID, GitHub, and local accounts
description: SpecGraph needs pluggable authentication supporting: (1) OIDC providers — Azure Entra ID and GitHub as first-class integrations with proper token validation, claims mapping, and group/role extraction. (2) Local account system — username/password or API key auth for environments where OIDC is unavailable or undesired. (3) Toggle mechanism — configuration to enable/disable OIDC entirely and fall back to local accounts, or run both side-by-side. Must include: provider discovery (.well-known/openid-configuration), token refresh, session management, RBAC mapping from OIDC claims, and comprehensive tests (unit tests with mock OIDC providers, integration tests against real Entra ID/GitHub test tenants). Consider: ConnectRPC interceptor for auth enforcement, config schema for provider registration, and graceful degradation when a provider is unreachable.
notes: JWT detection hook and IdentityStore interface in place. Ready for OIDC provider implementation.
close_reason: OIDC authentication implemented: multi-provider JWKS validation, claims mapping, auth modes (local/oidc/mixed), credential bootstrap, CompositeStore routing

title: Full text export/backup and restore with verification
description: SpecGraph needs a reliable backup/restore pipeline at two scopes: (1) Per-project export — dump all specs, decisions, edges, constitution layers for a single project as a portable text format (JSON, YAML, or custom). (2) Server-wide export — dump the entire server's graph data across all projects. Both scopes must include: restore functionality to import into a fresh or existing instance, and verification to confirm round-trip fidelity (export → restore → diff). Consider: format choice (human-readable vs compact), incremental vs full snapshots, streaming for large graphs, and integration with the storage interface so it works across backends (Memgraph today, alternatives per spgr-dag).
close_reason: PR #699 opened. Proto, storage, engine, handler, CLI all implemented with unit tests. Integration/E2E tests deferred (need Docker).

title: Evaluate alternative graph backends: Postgres+RDF, Neo4j, Nebula, JanusGraph
description: Memgraph is memory-constrained which limits scalability. Evaluate alternative storage backends that persist to disk: (1) PostgreSQL with RDF extensions (e.g. Apache AGE, pg_rdf), (2) Neo4j (mature Cypher, disk-based, ACID), (3) NebulaGraph (distributed, Cypher-compatible), (4) JanusGraph (pluggable backends like Cassandra/HBase), (5) other openCypher-compatible engines. Consider: query compatibility with existing Cypher queries, operational complexity, licensing, Go driver maturity, testcontainers support, and migration path from current Memgraph implementation.
close_reason: Consolidated into spgr-8nf — same investigation scope

title: Design: Default markdown output format for specs and related items
description: Design a standard markdown representation for specs and their related items (edges, decisions, constitution refs, etc.). Goals: (1) Human-readable + AI-consumable output from CLI commands — markdown is the normalized format for both audiences, (2) JSON stays for script/compute consumption (--json flag), (3) Consistent format across specgraph show, list, decision show, etc. Consider: section structure, metadata rendering, edge visualization, authoring funnel stage display, and how this format helps new users and AI agents build mental models of the spec graph.
close_reason: Implemented markdown CLI output: internal/render/ package with 8 entity renderers, --json flags on all 11 read commands, new findings list command. 17 commits, 27 render tests, task check passes.

title: Task 10: End-to-End Integration Test
description:
Write E2E test covering the full lifecycle flow through a running server.

## What to do
1. Create e2e/api/lifecycle_test.go following existing E2E test pattern
2. Test full lifecycle flow:
   - Create spec → move to done → amend → verify amended state
   - Create replacement spec → supersede original → verify superseded state
   - Create spec → abandon → verify abandoned state
   - Set up upstream/downstream deps → trigger drift → verify detected
   - Acknowledge drift → verify acknowledged
   - Run lint on valid spec → verify passed
3. Use existing E2E test infrastructure (testcontainers, Ginkgo/Gomega or standard testing)

## TDD Approach
E2E test exercises the full stack — proto → handler → memgraph → CLI. Requires Docker for Memgraph.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] E2E test covers create → done → amend flow
- [ ] E2E test covers supersede flow with SUPERSEDES edge
- [ ] E2E test covers abandon (terminal state)
- [ ] E2E test covers drift detection and acknowledgement
- [ ] E2E test covers lint validation
- [ ] go test ./e2e/api/ -run TestLifecycleE2E -v -count=1 -timeout=60s passes (with Docker)

## References
- Plan: docs/plans/2026-03-07-slice-5-spec-lifecycle-revised-plan.md (Task 10)
- E2E pattern: e2e/
close_reason: Task 10 complete: E2E lifecycle tests (amend, supersede, abandon, drift, drift ack, lint) with LifecycleService wired in testutil server.

title: Task 5: Spec JSON Schema + Schema Validator
description:
Create spec.schema.json and implement schema validation in the linter package using domain types.

## What to do
1. Create spec.schema.json at project root with JSON Schema draft 2020-12 validation rules
2. Write schema validator tests first (TDD): TestValidateSchema_ValidSpec, TestValidateSchema_MissingRequired, TestValidateSchema_InvalidStage, TestValidateSchema_SupersededWithoutBy, TestValidateSchema_SupersededWithBy, TestValidateSchema_InvalidPriority, TestValidateSchema_LivingLifecycle
3. Implement internal/linter/schema.go with ValidateSchema(*storage.Spec) []storage.LintViolation
4. Validates: required fields (slug, intent, stage), enum values (stage, priority, complexity, lifecycle), conditional (superseded requires superseded_by), version minimum

## TDD Approach
Write test cases BEFORE ValidateSchema implementation. All tests use *storage.Spec and storage.LintViolation domain types.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] spec.schema.json exists at project root with progressive validation rules
- [ ] ValidateSchema accepts *storage.Spec and returns []storage.LintViolation
- [ ] Missing required fields produce schema.required violations
- [ ] Invalid enum values produce schema.enum violations
- [ ] Superseded without superseded_by produces schema.conditional violation
- [ ] Valid specs produce no violations
- [ ] Zero proto imports in internal/linter/schema.go
- [ ] go test ./internal/linter/ -run TestValidateSchema passes

- Domain types: internal/storage/lifecycle.go (LintViolation, LintSeverity)

title: Use 'statement' instead of 'principle' for Principle proto field
description:
## Decision

The Principle proto message uses `statement` (field 2) instead of `principle` as specified in the Slice 2 plan doc.

## Rationale

- Avoids tautology of `Principle.principle`
- More descriptive: the field holds the principle's statement text
- Consistent with YAML struct `ConstitutionPrinciple.Statement`
- Already propagated correctly through all layers (emitter, CLI show, YAML bootstrap)

## Alternatives Considered

- **Keep 'principle' per plan**: Rejected — tautological naming (`Principle.principle`) is confusing and the plan is a design input, not a binding contract
- **Rename to 'text'**: Rejected — too generic, `statement` better conveys the field's purpose

## Affects

- [deleted:spgr-gk1.99] (PR #7 review finding about field name divergence)
- proto/specgraph/v1/constitution.proto — Principle message
- internal/config/config.go — ConstitutionPrinciple.Statement
close_reason: Decision already implemented — field is 'statement' in proto. This bead documents the rationale, no action needed.
